style_manifest.tone: tragic and sober, epic distance with intimate moments; violence without glorification; moral ambivalence toward empire and resistance
style_manifest.special_notes: Novel-to-feature compression: 46.290 words → 120 min. Original features a frame narrative (the thistle) — keep or cut is an explicit directorial choice, not default. Multi-POV novel (HM, Shamil, Russian court, HM's family) must be refocalized on HM as central protagonist; other POVs become relationships to him. 30+ named figures — fusion-candidates will be identified by Story Analyst + Graphify preprocessor. Historical figures (Shamil, Vorontsov, Nikolaus I.) are anchors, not fusable.
style_manifest.title: Hadji Murat

chunks.text:
I was returning home by the fields.  It was midsummer, the hay harvest was over and they were just beginning to reap the rye.  At that season of the year there is a delightful variety of flowers -- red, white, and pink scented tufty clover; milk-white ox-eye daisies with their bright yellow centers and pleasant spicy smell; yellow honey-scented rape blossoms; tall campanulas with white and lilac bells, tulip-shaped; creeping vetch; yellow, red, and pink scabious; faintly scented, neatly arranged purple plaintains with blossoms slightly tinged with pink; cornflowers, the newly opened blossoms bright blue in the sunshine but growing paler and redder towards evening or when growing old; and delicate almond-scented dodder flowers that withered quickly.  I gathered myself a large nosegay and was going home when I noticed in a ditch, in full bloom, a beautiful thistle plant of the crimson variety, which in our neighborhood they call "Tartar" and carefully avoid when mowing -- or, if they do happen to cut it down, throw out from among the grass for fear of pricking their hands.  Thinking to pick this thistle and put it in the center of my nosegay, I climbed down into the ditch, and after driving away a velvety bumble-bee that had penetrated deep into one of the flowers and had there fallen sweetly asleep, I set to work to pluck the flower. But this proved a very difficult task.  Not only did the stalk prick on every side -- even through the handkerchief I wrapped round my hand -- but it was so tough that I had to struggle with it for nearly five minutes, breaking the fibers one by one; and when I had at last plucked it, the stalk was all frayed and the flower itself no longer seemed so fresh and beautiful.  Moreover, owing to a coarseness and stiffness, it did not seem in place among the delicate blossoms of my nosegay.  I threw it away feeling sorry to have vainly destroyed a flower that looked beautiful in its proper place.

"But what energy and tenacity!  With what determination it defended itself, and how dearly it sold its life!" thought I, remembering the effort it had cost me to pluck the flower.  The way home led across black-earth fields that had just been ploughed up.  I ascended the dusty path.  The ploughed field belonged to a landed proprietor and was so large that on both sides and before me to the top of the hill nothing was visible but evenly furrowed and moist earth.  The land was well tilled and nowhere was there a blade of grass or any kind of

I ascended the dusty path.  The ploughed field belonged to a landed proprietor and was so large that on both sides and before me to the top of the hill nothing was visible but evenly furrowed and moist earth.  The land was well tilled and nowhere was there a blade of grass or any kind of plant to be seen, it was all black.  "Ah, what a destructive creature is man....How many different plant-lives he destroys to support his own existence!" thought I, involuntarily looking around for some living thing in this lifeless black field.  In front of me to the right of the road I saw some kind of little clump, and drawing nearer I found it was the same kind of thistle as that which I had vainly plucked and thrown away.  This "Tartar" plant had three branches.  One was broken and stuck out like the stump of a mutilated arm.  Each of the other two bore a flower, once red but now blackened. One stalk was broken, and half of it hung down with a soiled flower at its tip.  The other, though also soiled with black mud, still stood erect.  Evidently a cartwheel had passed over the plant but it had risen again, and that was why, though erect, it stood twisted to one side, as if a piece of its body had been torn from it, its bowels drawn out, an arm torn off, and one of its eyes plucked out.  Yet it stood firm and did not surrender to man who had destroyed all its brothers around it....

"What vitality!" I thought.  "Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one won't submit."  And I remembered a Caucasian episode of years ago, which I had partly seen myself, partly heard of from eye-witnesses, and in part imagined.

The episode, as it has taken shape in my memory and imagination, was as follows.

 



 

It happened towards the end of 1851.

On a cold November evening Hadji Murad rode into Makhmet, a hostile Chechen aoul that lay some fifteen miles from Russian territory and was filled with the scented smoke of burning Kizyak.  The strained chant of the muezzin had just ceased, and though the clear mountain air, impregnated with kizyak smoke, above the lowing of the cattle and the bleating of the sheep that were dispersing among the saklyas (which were crowded together like the cells of honeycomb), could be clearly heard the guttural voices of disputing men, and sounds of women's and children's voices rising from near the fountain below.

This Hadji Murad was Shamil's naib, famous for his exploits, who used never to ride out without his banne

This Hadji Murad was Shamil's naib, famous for his exploits, who used never to ride out without his banner and some dozens of murids, who caracoled and showed off before him.  Now wrapped in a hood and burka, from under which protruded a rifle, he rode, a fugitive with one murid only, trying to attract as little attention as possible and peering with his quick black eyes into the faces of those he met on his way.

When he entered the aoul, instead of riding up the road leading to the open square, he turned to the left into a narrow side street, and on reaching the second saklya, which was cut into the hill side, he stopped and looked round.  There was no one under the penthouse in front, but on the roof of the saklya itself, behind the freshly plastered clay chimney, lay a man covered with a sheepskin.  Hadji Murad touched him with the handle of his leather-plaited whip and clicked his tongue, and an old man, wearing a greasy old beshmet and a nightcap, rose from under the sheepskin.  His moist red eyelids had no lashes, and he blinked to get them unstuck.  Hadji Murad, repeating the customary "Selaam aleikum!" uncovered his face.  "aleikum, selaam!" said the old man, recognizing him, and smiling with his toothless mouth.  And raising himself on his thin legs he began thrusting his feet into the wooden-heeled slippers that stood by the chimney.  Then he leisurely slipped his arms into the sleeves of his crumpled sheepskin, and going to the ladder that leant against the roof he descended backwards, while he dressed and as he climbed down he kept shaking his head on its thin, shrivelled sunburnt neck and mumbling something with his toothless mouth.  As soon as he reached the ground he hospitably seized Hadji Murad's bridle and right stirrup; but the strong active murid had quickly dismounted and motioning the old man aside, took his place. Hadji Murad also dismounted, and walking with a slight limp, entered under the penthouse.  A boy of fifteen, coming quickly out of the door, met him and wonderingly fixed his sparkling eyes, black as ripe sloes, on the new arrivals.

"run to the mosque and call your father," ordered the old man as he hurried forward to open the thin, creaking door into the saklya.

As Hadji Murad entered the outer door, a slight, spare, middle-aged woman in a yellow smock, red beshmet, and wide blue trousers came through an inner door carrying cushions.

"May thy coming bring happiness!" said she, and bending nearly double began arranging

"run to the mosque and call your father," ordered the old man as he hurried forward to open the thin, creaking door into the saklya.

As Hadji Murad entered the outer door, a slight, spare, middle-aged woman in a yellow smock, red beshmet, and wide blue trousers came through an inner door carrying cushions.

"May thy coming bring happiness!" said she, and bending nearly double began arranging the cushions along the front wall for the guest to sit on.

"May thy sons live!" answered Hadji Murad, taking off his burka, his rifle, and his sword, and handing them to the old man who carefully hung the rifle and sword on a nail beside the weapons of the master of the house, which were suspended between two large basins that glittered against the clean clay-plastered and carefully whitewashed wall.

Hadji Murad adjusted the pistol at his back, came up to the cushions, and wrapping his Circassian coat closer round him, sat down.  The old man squatted on his bare heels beside him, closed his eyes, and lifted his hands palms upwards.  Hadji Murad did the same; then after repeating a prayer they both stroked their faces, passing their hands downwards till the palms joined at the end of their beards.

"Ne habar?" ("Is there anything new?") asked Hadji Murad, addressing the old man.

"Habar yok" ("Nothing new"), replied the old man, looking with his lifeless red eyes not at Hadji Murad's face but at his breast.  "I live at the apiary and have only today come to see my son....He knows."

Hadji Murad, understanding that the old man did not wish to say what he knew and what Hadji Murad wanted to know, slightly nodded his head and asked no more questions.

"There is no good news," said the old man.  "The only news is that the hares keep discussing how to drive away the eagles, and the eagles tear first one and then another of them. The other day the Russian dogs burnt the hay in the Mitchit aoul....May their faces be torn!" he added hoarsely and angrily.

Hadji Murad's murid entered the room, his strong legs striding softly over the earthen floor.  Retaining only his dagger and pistol, he took off his burka, rifle, and sword as Hadji Murad had done, and hung them up on the same nails as his leader's weapons.

"Who is he?" asked the old man, pointing to the newcomer.

"My murid.  Eldar is his name," said Hadji Murad.

"That is well," said the old man, and motioned Eldar to a place on a piece of felt beside Hadji Murad.  Eldar sat down, crossing his legs and fixing his fine r

"Who is he?" asked the old man, pointing to the newcomer.

"My murid.  Eldar is his name," said Hadji Murad.

"That is well," said the old man, and motioned Eldar to a place on a piece of felt beside Hadji Murad.  Eldar sat down, crossing his legs and fixing his fine ram-like eyes on the old man who, having now started talking, was telling how their brave fellows had caught two Russian soldiers the week before and had killed one and sent the other to Shamil in Veden.

Hadji Murad heard him absently, looking at the door and listening to the sounds outside.  Under the penthouse steps were heard, the door creaked, and Sado, the master of the house, came in.  He was a man of about forty, with a small beard, long nose, and eyes as black, though not as glittering, as those of his fifteen-year-old son who had run to call him home and who now entered with his father and sat down by the door.  The master of the house took off his wooden slippers at the door, and pushing his old and much-worn cap to the back of his head (which had remained unshaved so long that it was beginning to be overgrown with black hair), at once squatted down in front of Hadji Murad.

He too lifted his palms upwards, as the old man had done, repeated a prayer, and then stroked his face downwards.  Only after that did he begin to speak.  He told how an order had come from Shamil to seize Hadji Murad alive or dead, that Shamil's envoys had left only the day before, that the people were afraid to disobey Shamil's orders, and that therefore it was necessary to be careful.

"In my house," said Sado, "no one shall injure my kunak while I live, but how will it be in the open fields?...We must think it over."

Hadji Murad listened with attention and nodded approvingly.  When Sado had finished he said:

"Very well.  Now we must send a man with a letter to the Russians.  My murid will go but he will need a guide."

"I will send brother Bata," said Sado.  "Go and call Bata," he added, turning to his son.

The boy instantly bounded to his nimble feet as if he were on springs, and swinging his arms, rapidly left the saklya.  Some ten minutes later he returned with a sinewy, short-legged Chechen, burnt almost black by the sun, wearing a worn and tattered yellow Circassian coat with frayed sleeves, and crumpled black leggings.

Hadji Murad greeted the newcomer, and again without wasting a single word, immediately asked:

"Canst thou conduct my murid to the Russians?"

subplots.description: Hadji Murad rides into Makhmet disguised and accompanied by only one murid (Eldar), signalling that he has broken from Shamil and is now a fugitive.
Hadji Murad passes a note hidden in a cartridge pouch for Sado to forward to his son — first signal that his family is a driver behind his defection.
Hadji Murad passes a secret note for his son via Sado — first explicit reference to the family-rescue goal.
Sado accepts Hadji Murad as kunak despite Shamil's death-penalty edict — dramatises the conflict between the Chechen hospitality code (adat) and Shamil's religious-political authority (sharia-state).
Hadji Murad dispatches Bata as guide to lead Khan Mahoma and his murids through Argun forest to Russian commander Prince Vorontsov — the envoy mission is set in motion.
Russian ambush party at Vozdvizhensk: Panov, Avdeev, Nikitin, Bondarenko on night watch; cashbox complaint; Avdeev's homesickness monologue (went to war for his brother).

Hadji Murad asks 'Is there anything new?' The old man deflects — he says he has only come today and his son knows more. But then volunteers oblique, bitter news: the Russians burned hay in Mitchit Aou
Vorontsov's midnight drawing room: card game, champagne, Poltoratsky infatuated with Marya Vasilevna; Vorontsov receives envoy news; couple's excitement about Hadji Murad.
Narrator notices a crimson 'Tartar' thistle (locally called so; avoided by mowers) standing apart from the meadow flowers.
Vorontsov writes reports to General Kozlovsky and to his father — bureaucratic machinery activates around HM's defection; Vorontsov's jurisdictional anxiety about Meller-Zakomelsky surfaces.
Author-narrator walks home through midsummer fields and catalogues blooming wildflowers — establishing a pastoral, sensory prologue.

themes: Resistance unto death — the thistle sells its life dearly
Destruction of beauty through force — the plucked flower is ruined
The outsider who does not belong among the cultivated — thistle vs. garden flowers
Nature as moral commentary — pastoral frame precedes political violence
Imperial pressure on the individual — the mowers avoid or discard the thistle
Vitality under oppression — the thistle as embodied theme
Hospitality vs. political survival (kunak code vs. Shamil's edict)
Defection and betrayal as political acts with personal stakes
The individual crushed by imperial/theocratic machinery yet refusing to yield
Silence as resistance — Hadji Murad's economy of speech mirrors self-preservation
Fall from power — the once-magnificent naib reduced to a fugitive with one companion
Hospitality as political allegiance — welcoming Hadji Murad is a dangerous act of loyalty
Concealment and surveillance — Hadji Murad's wary scanning of faces, deliberate avoidance of the main road
Trust networks in occupied territory — knowing exactly which door to knock on
Pride stripped to survival — the banner and murids replaced by a burka and one man
Hospitality as sacred covenant (kunak bond)
Concealed knowledge — what is known but not spoken
Russian colonial violence rendered in a single offhand sentence ('they burned the hay')
Ritual as identity — prayer, disarmament, greeting phrases preserve Chechen selfhood under occupation
The fugitive within familiar space — safety is temporary, disguise still worn inside
Sacred hospitality vs. political survival (kunak code under Shamil's death order)
The human beneath the warrior: Hadji Murad's blush, his gentleness with Bulka, his childlike smile
Parallel worlds: Chechen kunak night / Russian card-and-champagne night — same stakes, different register
Avdeev as the human cost: the ordinary man who substituted for his brother, now shot for nothing
Defection as double betrayal: HM betrays Shamil, but also risks betraying his own people
Imperial machinery vs. human scale: soldiers' gossip, Vorontsov's jurisdictional anxiety, Marya Vasilevna's drawing room politics
Dreams and reality: HM dreams of capturing Shamil, wakes to jackals — desire vs. dependence

plot_elements.description: Author-narrator walks home through midsummer fields and catalogues blooming wildflowers — establishing a pastoral, sensory prologue.
Narrator notices a crimson 'Tartar' thistle (locally called so; avoided by mowers) standing apart from the meadow flowers.
Narrator attempts to pluck the thistle; struggles for five minutes against its prickles and tough stalk; finally tears it free — but the stem is frayed and the flower ruined.

Narrator discards the broken thistle and reflects: 'What energy and tenacity! With what determination it defended itself, and how dearly it sold its life!' — establishing the central metaphor for Hadji Murad's arc before the protagonist has appeared.
Narrator crosses a vast, freshly ploughed black-earth field — bare, without a single blade of grass — transitioning away from the flower world toward the novel's harsher terrain.
Author-narrator encounters a crushed but upright thistle on a ploughed field — its defiant survival triggers the memory of Hadji Murad. Establishes the central symbol and narrative frame.
Hadji Murad rides into Makhmet disguised and accompanied by only one murid (Eldar), signalling that he has broken from Shamil and is now a fugitive.
Hadji Murad seeks shelter at Sado's house; old man delivers news that Shamil has issued a kill-or-capture order on Hadji Murad to all Chechens.
Sado accepts Hadji Murad as kunak despite Shamil's death-penalty edict — dramatises the conflict between the Chechen hospitality code (adat) and Shamil's religious-political authority (sharia-state).
Hadji Murad dispatches Bata as guide to lead Khan Mahoma and his murids through Argun forest to Russian commander Prince Vorontsov — the envoy mission is set in motion.
Hadji Murad passes a note hidden in a cartridge pouch for Sado to forward to his son — first signal that his family is a driver behind his defection.
Old man's proverb: 'the hares keep discussing how to drive away the eagles' — veiled commentary on Chechen resistance; Russian raid on Mitchit aoul mentioned.
Hadji Murad, formerly Shamil's naib with dozens of murids, now rides as a fugitive with only one murid, concealed under hood and burka, scanning faces warily.
Hadji Murad enters the aoul via a narrow side street, avoiding the main road — deliberate evasion that underscores fugitive status and instinct for concealment.
Hadji Murad stops at a specific saklya, wakes the old man with his whip handle — indicating prior knowledge of a trusted contact or safe house in this village.
Old man immediately recognises Hadji Murad and welcomes him warmly, confirming he has a network of loyal supporters even in his fallen state.
Boy is sent to fetch his father from the mosque — the household head is absent, suggesting Hadji Murad will need to negotiate or wait for the key decision-maker.

Woman of the house greets Hadji Murad with the traditional phrase 'May thy coming bring happiness' and begins arranging cushions — conventional hospitality ritual that implicitly accepts him into the saklya.

Hadji Murad enters the saklya of the old man — a trusted contact in the aoul. Weapons are ceremonially hung on the wall; host and guest perform Islamic prayer together. Ritual of kunak (blood-brotherhood) hospitality established.

Hadji Murad asks 'Is there anything new?' The old man deflects — he says he has only come today and his son knows more. But then volunteers oblique, bitter news: the Russians burned hay in Mitchit Aoul. The old man's coded speech (eagles vs. hares) establishes the oppressive political atmosphere.
Eldar is introduced as named murid accompanying Hadji Murad on the covert mission. He mirrors his leader's ritual gestures exactly, reinforcing the loyalty-and-discipline theme.
Hadji Murad arrives at Sado's saklya with murid Eldar; Sado reveals Shamil has ordered his capture dead or alive; kunak bond declared.
Hadji Murad dispatches Bata as guide to lead Khan Mahoma through Russian lines to Prince Vorontsov with his surrender offer.
Sado's wife and daughter serve food; silent gendered ritual; Eldar stone-still while women present — cultural texture of Chechen hospitality.
Hadji Murad passes a secret note for his son via Sado — first explicit reference to the family-rescue goal.
Sado knowingly risks death to shelter Hadji Murad; his kunak duty overrides fear of Shamil — dramatizes the moral weight of the kunak code.
Midnight warning: whole aoul knows Hadji Murad is here; Elders assemble in mosque to detain him. Sado wakes him.
Hadji Murad and Eldar make a night escape from the aoul on horseback; aoul men pursue but are bluffed off by raised rifle.
Hadji Murad reaches murids' forest camp; meets Khanefi, Gamzalo; prays; dreams of capturing Shamil and ruling Chechnya — exposes his hidden ambition and longing.
Khan Mahoma returns: Vorontsov promises to receive Hadji Murad well tomorrow at the Shalin glade wood-felling site.
Russian ambush party at Vozdvizhensk: Panov, Avdeev, Nikitin, Bondarenko on night watch; cashbox complaint; Avdeev's homesickness monologue (went to war for his brother).
Bata and Khan Mahoma intercepted by ambush soldiers; Avdeev connects humanly with the scouts; they are delivered safely to the fort.
Vorontsov's midnight drawing room: card game, champagne, Poltoratsky infatuated with Marya Vasilevna; Vorontsov receives envoy news; couple's excitement about Hadji Murad.
Wood-felling morning: Poltoratsky's officers eat and joke about General Sleptsov's death — ironic heroism myth, indifference to actual violence.
Chechen pursuers fire on Russian line; Poltoratsky orders volley; Avdeev is shot in the stomach — mortal wound during a pointless skirmish.
Vorontsov rides up; tells Poltoratsky Hadji Murad is about to surrender; Poltoratsky deploys sharpshooters to escort route.

FORMAL SURRENDER: Hadji Murad and five murids ride out of forest and meet Vorontsov. Hadji Murad speaks his surrender formula: 'I surrender to the Russian Tsar. I wished to do so long ago but Shamil would not let me.' Handshake; retinue introduced.
Soldiers comment on Hadji Murad: mix of admiration and resentment — 'What a lot of men that damned fellow has destroyed!' vs. 'He's a fine fellow — a real dzhigit!'
Hadji Murad brought to Vorontsov's house; Marya Vasilevna receives him; he blushes at her hand; sits with little Bulka on his knee — human tenderness behind the warrior mask.
Vorontsov writes reports to General Kozlovsky and to his father — bureaucratic machinery activates around HM's defection; Vorontsov's jurisdictional anxiety about Meller-Zakomelsky surfaces.
Kunak-gift exchange conversation: Marya Vasilevna teases HM that he'll go about like Adam; HM explains the kunak gift law. Light humor as political ice-breaker.

symbols: Crimson 'Tartar' thistle — Hadji Murad; fierce, beautiful, ultimately destroyed
Nosegay of cultivated flowers — Russian imperial society, polished and fragile
The ditch — margin, exile, the space outside accepted order
Frayed stalk — the cost of resistance; victory that destroys what it defends
Black-earth ploughed field — stripped land, imperial control, erasure of wildness
The Tartar thistle — compressed, broken, yet upright; direct visual analogue of Hadji Murad
The cartridge pouch hiding the letter — concealment of personal love beneath military armour
Weapons hung side by side on Sado's wall — equality of guest and host; Chechen code materialised
Honey — abundance, goodness, domestic peace set against violence outside
Hood and burka — concealment of former glory
The slight limp — physical mark of past battles and vulnerability
Single murid vs. dozens — numerical reduction as dramatisation of political fall
Rifle protruding from burka — concealed danger beneath disguise
Boy's sloe-black eyes — innocence and wonder at a living legend
Cushions being arranged — domesticity and safety contrasting the fugitive exterior
Weapons hung between gleaming copper basins — martial and domestic life inseparable
Pistol kept at Hadji Murad's back even inside the sanctuary — trust has limits
Ritual prayer with palms upward — submission to God, not to Russia
Eagles and hares — the old man's fable: Chechens are hares, Russians are eagles
Kunak bond — sacred obligation that overrides self-preservation
Stars and the Pleiades — HM checks them mid-prayer; time passing, fate marking
Rifle raised in darkness — power through threat, not violence; HM's authority without bloodshed
Avdeev's wound in the stomach — the gut-shot of pointless war, the body as price of abstraction
Hadji Murad on white-maned horse with gold ornaments — the legend made visible for the first time
Bulka on HM's knee — innocence as bridge between enemies
Honey at Sado's table — sweetness and calm before the danger

analysis_notes.chunk_001: Chunk 1 is the author-frame prologue — Tolstoy's personal voice, no dramatic action, no named historical characters. The thistle metaphor is the entire load-bearing content of this chunk: it announces Hadji Murad's fate (resistance, ruin, dearly sold life) before he appears. The chunk is short and functions as a cinematic title-card equivalent. Filmability is moderate (0.55) rather than low because the imagery is highly visual and a skilled director can render it without dialogue — but the inner reflection ('What energy and tenacity!') requires either V.O. or visual metaphor translation. The pre-computed graph confirms no fusion candidates appear here; no cuttable-list characters appear. The author_digression and thistle_frame plotline threads appear together and are flagged for fusion per the Plotline Graph hint.
analysis_notes.chunk_002: This chunk is the narrative hinge: the thistle-frame prologue (authorial digression, Chapter-I equivalent) transitions into the story proper. Chunk covers approximately Chapters I–II of the novel. Shamil's kill-order is already active, meaning the defection has already occurred before this chunk begins — Chunk 1 likely established the wider context. The kunak relationship with Sado is load-bearing (must-keep thread). Bata is an efficient single-use operative. The narrator's 'I' exists only in the prologue and can be dissolved entirely in film — the thistle can open the film as a pure visual image without voice-over.
analysis_notes.chunk_003: Chunk 3 is a tightly cinematic scene of arrival and concealment. The passage contrasts Hadji Murad's former grandeur (banner, dozens of murids) with his present abjection (one murid, hood, burka, limp) in a single economical paragraph — a compression gift for the film. The safe-house sequence is wordless in action: waking the old man, the boy sent off, the woman greeting. Almost no inner monologue; everything is exteriorised behaviour. Highly filmable as-is. The unnamed old man, boy, and woman are atmospheric supporting figures with no independent arc — classic cuttable or purely functional presences. The village/aoul itself overlaps with the 'Sado's Aoul' location cluster (cinematic anchor, weight=10) and may be the same or an earlier stop. Needs confirmation in subsequent chunks. The chunk ends mid-sentence ('and bending nearly double began arranging') — scene continues into chunk 4.
analysis_notes.chunk_004: This chunk is extremely short (~300 words) and overlaps with the tail of Chunk 3 (the 'run to the mosque' line is the bridging sentence). The dramatic content is almost entirely atmospheric and ritual: no plot advancement beyond naming Eldar and establishing the saklya as a refuge node. The chunk functions as a breathing pause — Hadji Murad briefly safe, gathering intelligence. The old man's information-withholding followed by the oblique 'Mitchit Aoul' disclosure is a classic Tolstoyan indirection technique: what is NOT said carries the weight. Filmically, this is a strong visual scene (the gleaming basins, the weapons ritual, the prayer), but it lacks dramatic stakes on its own. It gains weight only in context of HM's hunted status (Chunk 3). In compression mode, this scene can be folded into the broader arrival sequence — the saklya interior need not be a standalone scene but can be compressed into a single INTERCUT or follow-on beat after the arrival. The Mitchit Aoul detail is a cuttable line of dialogue that can be dropped or absorbed into a wider Russian raid motif already established. Eldar's naming here is load-bearing — he must be introduced somewhere before he functions as a named murid later.
analysis_notes.chunk_005: This is the pivotal structural hinge of the entire novella: the surrender. Chapters I–VI deliver setup (Chechen night), complication (aoul escape, envoy mission), parallel subplot (Russian soldiers' night watch), and the climactic Act 1 Turning Point — Hadji Murad formally crosses to the Russian side. Avdeev's wounding here is dramaturgically crucial: it ties the Chechen political drama to a specific human cost at the exact moment of the 'good news' (the surrender). The chunk has exceptional filmability — nearly all scenes are visual action or charged dialogue. The Sado kunak-hospitality section and the surrender itself are cinematic setpieces. Marya Vasilevna and Poltoratsky both appear here fully; their subplot is entirely cuttable in compression. Gamzalo is introduced as a menace — his later role (staying loyal to Shamil's worldview among HM's murids) is seeded here by his refusal to look at Vorontsov.

voice_profile.voice_description: Hadji Murad speaks in short, functional sentences — every word is an action or a probe, never ornament. His register moves between the ritualized formality of Islamic and Chechen greeting (elevated, cadenced, near-liturgical) and the clipped imperative of a commander assigning a task. Silence is his primary rhetorical tool: he listens with his eyes on the door, and when he speaks it is to decide.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: Formal-plain. Educated in religious and military tradition, not in courtly rhetoric. Uses Chechen and Arabic-Islamic formulas (Selaam aleikum, Ne habar, Habar yok) in greetings and questions. Archaic second person in English rendering (thou, thy, canst) for gravitas; no abstract, sentimental, or introspective vocabulary.
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Average 5–10 words. Predominantly declarative and imperative. Questions are direct and singular (never compound). Fragments appear under pressure. Almost never uses subordinate clauses — thought moves by juxtaposition, not by hinging.
verbal_tics: Opens exchanges with ritual greeting before any content ('Selaam aleikum', 'May thy sons live')
Asks 'Ne habar?' (Is there anything new?) as standing probe for intelligence
Introduces subordinates by function and name in one beat ('My murid. Eldar is his name.')
'Now we must —' as pivot from listening to decision
Nods once instead of acknowledging verbally
avoidances: Never explains his feelings — no 'I am afraid', 'I am tired', 'I miss'
Never complains about Shamil, the Russians, or fate — grievance is beneath a naib
Never uses abstract or philosophical language ('freedom', 'destiny', 'honor' as concepts) — honor is shown in conduct, not named
Never begs, pleads, or negotiates in emotional register — requests are framed as assignments or questions
Never interrupts an elder; never presses a host who has signaled reticence
Never swears, never jokes loosely, never uses diminutives or endearments even with his murid
Never speaks of his family by name in front of strangers — they exist as silence
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling is carried almost entirely in body and tempo: the slight limp, a hand closing on the hilt of the sword, closed eyes during prayer, listening past the speaker to sounds outside the door. Verbal emotion appears only as compression — a shortened sentence, a pivot mid-exchange, a name spoken once. Love and grief for his family surface only in ritual phrasing ('May thy sons live') displaced onto others.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: Measured with sudden accelerations. Long stillnesses of listening, then a cluster of imperative sentences that resolve a decision. Never hectic, never meandering.

subtext_rules.when_he_says_Ne_habar_he_means: Is Shamil's hand already on my family, and is this house still safe for me tonight?
subtext_rules.when_he_nods_slightly_he_means: I have heard what you refused to say, and I will not force you to say it.
subtext_rules.when_he_greets_formally_he_means: I am placing myself under the protection of your hearth; betrayal of me is now betrayal of your house.
subtext_rules.when_he_gives_an_order_without_preamble_: The situation is worse than I will tell you; act now so I do not have to explain why.
subtext_rules.when_he_falls_silent_mid_conversation_he: I am listening for horses; continue talking so the room sounds normal.
subtext_rules.when_he_speaks_of_the_Russians_as_a_dest: I have no allies left; I am choosing the lesser captor.

psychology.want: To reach the Russians alive, secure their protection, and use their power to free his family from Shamil and return to take revenge — a concrete, operational goal driving every scene.
psychology.need: To recognize that no sovereign — neither Shamil nor the Tsar — will treat him as a man rather than an instrument; his need is the acceptance of sovereign solitude, the warrior's dignity that does not depend on a patron.
psychology.fear: That his family (mother, wives, sons) will be seized or killed by Shamil before he can act — and beneath that, the fear of dying as a pawn in another man's war.
psychology.contradiction: A legendary warrior who travels disguised and silent; a man of Islamic devotion who prepares to ally with Christian occupiers; a commander who commands murids yet defers with reverence to an old apiarist and accepts indirect answers without pressing.

arc.act_1_start: Famous naib reduced to fugitive — arrives at night in a hostile aoul with one murid, still carrying the posture and authority of command but stripped of banner and escort. Believes strategy and alliance can save his family.
arc.act_2_change: Enters Russian orbit — courted, watched, measured. Learns that the Russian court treats him as a trophy, not an ally; that Vorontsov's courtesy and Nikolaus's indifference are two faces of the same empire. Family remains hostage; every diplomatic path narrows.
arc.act_3_end: Breaks out in a final ride — chooses death in motion over captivity in either court. Dies outnumbered, severed from both worlds, having refused to be anyone's instrument. Head paraded; legend intact; man erased.

voice_profile.voice_description: Eldar speaks rarely, and when he does, his words are short, concrete, and stripped of ornament. His sentences are declarative or affirmative — he answers, he confirms, he reports; he does not speculate, argue, or soften. His register is that of a young mountain warrior trained in deference: grave, respectful, unembellished, with the weight of silence around every word.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal, oral, uneducated in a scholarly sense but fluent in the formal courtesies of the Caucasian warrior code; 19th-century highland idiom; no abstract or political vocabulary

sentence_structure.complexity: simple, predominantly single-clause; occasional coordinated clauses joined by 'and'
sentence_structure.tendency: statement and affirmation dominant; questions only when strictly functional; fragments common under stress or in acknowledgement

verbal_tics: 'Yes' / 'It is so' as first word of most replies
addresses Hadji Murad by honorific ('agha', 'my lord') before content
invokes 'Allah' or 'by Allah' sparingly, only at moments of oath or gravity
repeats the last significant word of an order back as confirmation ('The letter. Yes.')
avoidances: never contradicts Hadji Murad, even implicitly
never complains about fatigue, hunger, wounds, or fear
never speaks of himself in the first person at length — no autobiography, no feelings volunteered
never jokes in the presence of his leader or a host
never uses Russian loanwords or imperial vocabulary
never boasts of his own strength or skill
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings surface through the body, not the tongue — a fixed gaze, a tightened jaw, a hand moving to the dagger hilt. Verbal expression of emotion is almost absent; what he feels must be read from his eyes and his stillness. When moved, he grows more silent, not more voluble.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured and sparse — long silences between short utterances; pace does not quicken under pressure, it compresses

subtext_rules: When he says 'Yes, agha' without elaboration, he has understood an order he would rather not carry out — and will carry it out anyway.
When he falls completely silent in a room, he is assessing threat and calculating the first strike.
When he repeats an order back verbatim, he is binding himself to it as an oath.
When he says 'I will go', he means 'I will die before I fail to go.'
When he speaks of Hadji Murad in the third person to strangers, the formality is protection — he is making his leader larger than the room.

eldar.physical_signature: fine ram-like eyes — steady, unblinking, fixing on whoever speaks; paired with a soft-footed stride on strong legs that mutes his physical weight

basic_data.social_status: murid — devoted disciple and bodyguard of Hadji Murad; warrior class, subordinate to his leader
basic_data.physical_signature: fine ram-like eyes — steady, unblinking, fixing on whoever speaks; paired with a soft-footed stride on strong legs that mutes his physical weight

psychology.want: To serve Hadji Murad faultlessly — to be where his leader is, to carry what his leader entrusts him with, to mirror him exactly.
psychology.need: To discover an identity that exists apart from his leader — a self that survives if Hadji Murad falls.
psychology.fear: Failing Hadji Murad — through a wrong word, a wrong gesture, or a moment of hesitation that shames his leader in another man's house.
psychology.contradiction: He is physically formidable and armed, yet makes himself small in every room — the strongest body in the saklya occupies the least space and speaks least.

arc.act_1: Silent shadow of Hadji Murad — mirrors every gesture, speaks only when directly addressed, exists as an extension of his leader's body.
arc.act_2: Carries independent responsibility (the letter to the Russians); is forced into rooms and exchanges where Hadji Murad is not present to copy.
arc.act_3: Remains loyal past the point loyalty can save anyone — his last act is a murid's act, defined by the man he served rather than by himself.

voice_profile.voice_description: Eldar speaks rarely, and when he does, his words are short, concrete, and stripped of ornament. His sentences are declarative or affirmative — he answers, he confirms, he reports; he does not speculate, argue, or soften. His register is that of a young mountain warrior trained in deference: grave, respectful, unembellished, with the weight of silence around every word.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal, oral, uneducated in a scholarly sense but fluent in the formal courtesies of the Caucasian warrior code; 19th-century highland idiom; no abstract or political vocabulary

sentence_structure.complexity: simple, predominantly single-clause; occasional coordinated clauses joined by 'and'
sentence_structure.tendency: statement and affirmation dominant; questions only when strictly functional; fragments common under stress or in acknowledgement

verbal_tics: 'Yes' / 'It is so' as first word of most replies
addresses Hadji Murad by honorific ('agha', 'my lord') before content
invokes 'Allah' or 'by Allah' sparingly, only at moments of oath or gravity
repeats the last significant word of an order back as confirmation ('The letter. Yes.')
avoidances: never contradicts Hadji Murad, even implicitly
never complains about fatigue, hunger, wounds, or fear
never speaks of himself in the first person at length — no autobiography, no feelings volunteered
never jokes in the presence of his leader or a host
never uses Russian loanwords or imperial vocabulary
never boasts of his own strength or skill
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feelings surface through the body, not the tongue — a fixed gaze, a tightened jaw, a hand moving to the dagger hilt. Verbal expression of emotion is almost absent; what he feels must be read from his eyes and his stillness. When moved, he grows more silent, not more voluble.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured and sparse — long silences between short utterances; pace does not quicken under pressure, it compresses

voice_profile.voice_description: Sado speaks in short, weighted declarations that arrive only after ritual preparation — the prayer, the stroked face, the settled squat. His speech is grave, older than its speaker, shaped by Chechen hospitality-code and the cadence of men who do not waste breath. When he speaks, the room listens, because each sentence is either a vow, a report, or a command, never decoration.
voice_profile.vocabulary_level: informal but formal-in-weight; uneducated in the bookish sense, deeply schooled in custom and religious formula; 19th-century Caucasian village register
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short to medium clauses. Statements and brief imperatives dominate; one rhetorical question per crisis. Average 8–14 words. Compound sentences only when contrasting inside-house and open-field, a signature antithesis.
verbal_tics: Opens grave speech with a prayer-gesture rather than words
Uses 'my house' / 'my kunak' as anchoring possessives
Addresses kin by role before name ('brother Bata', 'my son')
Reaches for the conditional 'we must' when a decision is unavoidable
avoidances: Boasting, flattery, or poetic flourish
Complaints about personal cost
Questioning a guest's honor or plans
Speaking of fear in the first person
Rapid, unconsidered promises
voice_profile.emotional_expression: Feeling is displaced into ritual and action — the prayer, the cap-gesture, the dispatch of a brother. Warmth shows as provision; alarm shows as the single sober admission ('we must think it over'). He does not name emotions; he performs obligations that imply them.
voice_profile.speech_rhythm: measured, with deliberate pauses between clauses; silence carries as much weight as speech

example_dialogues.under_pressure: In my house, no one shall injure my kunak while I live. But the open fields — we must think it over.
example_dialogues.hopeful: I will send brother Bata. He knows every gully from here to the Russian line.
example_dialogues.angry_hurt: Shamil's envoys rode out yesterday. The people fear him. That is all I will say of them.
example_dialogues.vulnerable_honest: A man's roof is narrow. Beyond it I cannot answer for you — only ride with you, if it comes to that.

subtext_rules.when_he_offers_bata_he_means: I am committing my own blood to your survival, not just my word

sado.physical_signature: The old worn cap pushed back on a head where the long-unshaved scalp has only just begun to regrow black hair — a man who has been traveling hard, returning home only to shoulder risk again. The gesture of pushing the cap back precedes every serious utterance.

basic_data.social_status: Master of his own household; village man of modest means; respected as kunak-host; has a brother (Bata) and a fifteen-year-old son
basic_data.physical_signature: The old worn cap pushed back on a head where the long-unshaved scalp has only just begun to regrow black hair — a man who has been traveling hard, returning home only to shoulder risk again. The gesture of pushing the cap back precedes every serious utterance.

psychology.want: To honor the kunak-bond and shelter Hadji Murad safely under his roof, and to get him out of Chechen-Aul alive before Shamil's men arrive.
psychology.need: To live in accordance with the ancient code even when the world around him (Shamil, the people's fear) has abandoned it — to prove that one honest man still keeps his word.
psychology.fear: That he cannot protect his guest beyond the threshold of his house — that honor inside the saklya is meaningless if the open fields betray it. Secondarily: that his family will pay for his loyalty.
psychology.contradiction: He is emphatic and absolute about protection inside his walls ('no one shall injure my kunak while I live') yet immediately admits helplessness outside them ('how will it be in the open fields?'). Courage and pragmatism coexist without resolution.

arc.act_1_start: Offstage — a known kunak of Hadji Murad, his house a presumed refuge.
arc.act_2_change: Returns to find his guest hunted by Shamil's decree; performs the hospitality-ritual, pledges his house, then pivots to action: sends his brother Bata as guide. Accepts the weight of the decision without theatrics.
arc.act_3_end: Remains the quiet anchor of the Chechen-Aul chapter — a man who kept his word at personal risk. Does not transform; he endures.

old_man_(apiarist).initial_description: Elderly Chechen, Sado's relative; moist red lashless eyes; lives at an apiary; delivers veiled news about Shamil's order in proverbial language ('the hares and the eagles').

sado's_son.initial_description: Boy of about fifteen, sloe-black eyes; runs to call his father; gazes at Hadji Murad throughout with wide-eyed admiration.

bata.initial_description: Short-legged, sun-burnt Chechen guide; boastful but capable; agrees to escort Eldar (or Khan Mahoma's murid) to Russian commander Vorontsov in exchange for three coins — though he claims honour is enough.

sado's_wife.initial_description: Thin middle-aged woman in yellow smock and red beshmet; arranges cushions and brings food; silent, deferential.

sado's_daughter.initial_description: Young girl in red trousers and green beshmet; silver coin necklace; tries to look stern; does not look at male guests — traditional Chechen modesty.

khan_mahoma.initial_description: One of Hadji Murad's four murids waiting at the Argun glade; entrusted with delivering Hadji Murad's message to Prince Vorontsov; appears only by name in this chunk.

author-narrator_(i).initial_description: First-person frame narrator; a traveller who discovers the battered thistle on a ploughed field and uses it as the trigger to 'remember' the Hadji Murad episode.

old_man_(unnamed_host).initial_description: Elderly Chechen villager with moist red lashless eyelids, greasy beshmet, nightcap. Wakened from sleep on rooftop. Hospitable, immediately recognises Hadji Murad.

boy_(host's_grandson/son).initial_description: Fifteen-year-old with sparkling black eyes likened to ripe sloes. Sent to fetch his father from the mosque.

woman_of_the_house_(unnamed).initial_description: Slight, spare, middle-aged woman in yellow smock, red beshmet, wide blue trousers. Greets Hadji Murad with traditional welcome phrase, begins arranging cushions.

murid_(unnamed,_single_companion).initial_description: Strong, active murid who accompanies Hadji Murad in his fugitive state. Displaces the old man from holding Hadji Murad's bridle and stirrup.

old_man's_wife.initial_description: Slight, middle-aged Chechen woman in yellow smock, red beshmet, wide blue trousers. Performs the traditional hostess role: lays out cushions, greets with ritual phrase. Unnamed.

khanefi.initial_description: Avar murid; thick-set, hairy, eyebrows meeting; manages all of Hadji Murad's property and pack animals; sworn brother who manages HM's household.

gamzalo.initial_description: Chechen murid; short red beard, no eyebrows or eyelashes, blind in one eye, scar across nose and face; silent, menacing guard; instinctively takes defensive position without orders.

panov.initial_description: Non-commissioned officer at Vozdvizhensk; long-suffering, pragmatic, fair; leads the ambush party; breaks up soldiers' bickering about the cashbox.

avdeev.initial_description: Russian soldier; cheerful, homesick, went to war voluntarily in place of his brother; forms easy human connection even with Chechen scouts; mortally wounded in the skirmish.

nikitin.initial_description: Russian soldier; morose, distrustful; grumbles about the company commander's cashbox theft; suspicious of Chechens.

bondarenko.initial_description: Russian soldier, third man in ambush party; Ukrainian, cheerful voice; accompanies Avdeev to deliver the scouts to the fort.

simon_vorontsov.initial_description: Prince, Commander of Kurin Regiment, Imperial Aide-de-Camp, son of Commander-in-Chief; long-faced, English-accented Russian; politically ambitious, pleased to secure Hadji Murad's defection before his superior Meller-Zakomelsky can.

marya_vasilevna.initial_description: Princess, Vorontsov's wife; large-built, black-browed Petersburg beauty; warm, quick-minded, flirtatious with Poltoratsky; immediately charmed by Hadji Murad's blush.

poltoratsky.initial_description: Company commander, ex-Guard; wide-set kindly eyes; in a state of elation near danger; infatuated with Marya Vasilevna; first Russian to meet Hadji Murad face-to-face; surprised by his childlike smile.

baron_freze.initial_description: Ex-Guards officer reduced to ranks for duelling; Poltoratsky's fellow student at Cadet College; present at the wood-felling officers' breakfast.

tikhonov.initial_description: Poltoratsky's subaltern and roommate; brief appearance.

bulka.initial_description: Marya Vasilevna's curly-haired six-year-old son from her first marriage; sits on Hadji Murad's knee; symbol of the unexpected human gentleness in the conqueror.

regimental_adjutant.initial_description: Sits straight, cold handsome face; stern card partner; procedural figure in Vorontsov's household.

tutor.initial_description: Shaggy young Petersburg University graduate sent to tutor Bulka; gloomy appearance; Vorontsov's card partner.

locations.name: EXT. MIDSUMMER MEADOW / FLOWER FIELD - DAY
locations.cinematic_description: Wide establishing shot: rolling meadow in peak midsummer light, shallow depth of field pulling focus through layers of wildflowers. Color palette rich and saturated — blues, reds, whites, yellows. A lone figure (the Narrator) moves through the field collecting flowers. Natural golden-hour or high noon light. Handheld camera, slow drift. The world is abundant, unguarded, alive.
locations.atmosphere: Pastoral abundance; deceptive serenity before violence; the beauty that will be destroyed. Functions as ironic counterpoint to the story of Hadji Murat — a frame of natural grace enclosing imperial brutality.
locations.timeframe: Frame narrative — pre-Act I / prologue. Midsummer, daytime.
locations.narrative_function: Ironic prologue; establishes the thistle-as-Hadji-Murat motif; chromatic peak before the story strips color away
production_notes: Wildflower palette: crimson, white, blue, lilac, yellow — maximum chromatic richness
Thistle (crimson 'Tartar' variety) must be practical prop, prominently placed in ditch
Bumble-bee on flower: undisturbed natural world, quiet life of small creatures
Nosegay of delicate flowers contrasts with single, tough thistle — central visual motif
Ditch where thistle grows slightly recessed from the main meadow — isolates the thistle spatially
Late-season feel: hay already cut, rye just beginning — transitional moment, end of something

locations.name: EXT. BLACK-EARTH PLOUGHED FIELD - DAY
locations.cinematic_description: Cut from the flower field to stark, desolate ploughed earth. Wide shot: the Narrator a small figure on a dust-white path crossing endless dark furrows to a hill horizon. No colour, no life — only uniformity. Drone or crane shot to emphasise scale and erasure of nature. Dust rises from the path.
locations.atmosphere: Erasure, domination, order imposed on nature. Visual echo of empire's logic: the landed proprietor's will made visible in the land. Transition space between the living world and the dead. Pre-figures Russian imperial flattening of Chechen culture.
locations.timeframe: Frame narrative — prologue, directly after the meadow, midsummer, daytime. Also: second thistle encounter (crushed by cartwheel) triggers the main story.
locations.narrative_function: Visual bridge from pastoral prologue to main narrative; thistle-crushed-but-erect triggers memory of Hadji Murat; empire-as-landowner motif established
production_notes: Dusty path cutting through the field — worn, cartwheel tracks visible
Overcast or harsh midday light — no romantic golden hour
All-black soil, no green visible anywhere except the thistle
Thistle: three branches, one broken, two bearing blackened flowers, soiled with mud but erect
Path is dusty-white against the dark earth — strong graphic contrast for wide shots
Black earth (chernozem): deep brown-black, moist, freshly turned — high contrast against dusty path
Scale of the field emphasises the proprietor's ownership — land as political statement

locations.name: EXT. MAKHMET AOUL - NIGHT
locations.cinematic_description: Establishing shot from ridge: the aoul as a tight cluster of stone saklyas on a mountainside, smoke rising from every roof in the cold air. Blue-black night sky, mountain peaks behind. Cut to street level: Hadji Murat on horseback moving through narrow lanes, his silhouette lit by the ember-glow from doorways. Sound design dense — cattle, sheep, voices, silence of the muezzin just ended. The village feels enclosed, watchful, hostile.
locations.atmosphere: Tense arrival. The aoul is hostile territory — for Russians and potentially for Hadji Murat both. Cold mountain night, kizyak smoke as sensory marker of inhabited danger. Epic scale with intimate unease.
locations.narrative_function: First appearance of the Chechen world; establishes the political geography (15 miles from Russian territory); introduces Hadji Murat's fugitive status
production_notes: Kizyak smoke: dense, aromatic, low-hanging — practical smoke effect, not digital
Saklyas packed tight 'like cells of honeycomb' — cramped stone architecture, narrow lanes
Livestock dispersing through streets — practical animals needed
Cold breath visible on actors and horses
Color palette: ochre stone, black shadow, orange ember-glow from interiors
Reference: Come and See for oppressive village atmosphere; Lawrence of Arabia for mountain arrival scale

locations.name: EXT. CHECHEN AOUL SIDE STREET - DAY
locations.cinematic_description: Tight, shadowed alley between stone saklyas carved into a hillside. Low angle on Hadji Murat's horse moving cautiously. His eyes scan doorways and rooftops. The street is deserted — a deliberate choice by a man who knows what surveillance looks like.
locations.atmosphere: Paranoia and concealment. A fugitive threading the needle between visibility and safety. The narrowness of the street mirrors his narrowing options.
locations.timeframe: Act 1 — daytime, arrival sequence at the aoul
locations.narrative_function: Spatial compression; HM avoids main square — establishes his tactical thinking and knowledge of the village layout
production_notes: Stone and clay construction, buildings carved directly into hillside — organic, fortress-like quality
Penthouse (covered porch/overhang) in front of the saklya is key architectural element
Freshly plastered clay chimney — domestic life amid danger
Sheepskin as universal prop: bedding, cloak, marker of poverty and simplicity

locations.name: EXT. OLD MAN'S SAKLYA ROOF - DAY
locations.cinematic_description: Flat roof, midday heat. An old man asleep behind a chimney — a sentinel who has stopped watching. Hadji Murat taps him awake with a whip handle. The contrast between the dormant old man and Hadji Murat's coiled alertness captures the scene's tension.
locations.atmosphere: Fragile safety. The trust network Hadji Murat relies on is aging and slow. A quiet dread beneath the mundane.
locations.timeframe: Act 1 — daytime, arrival sequence (continuous with loc_004)
locations.narrative_function: First contact in the village; the old man's dormancy signals the community's vulnerability; roof as liminal space between public and private
production_notes: Wooden-heeled slippers by the chimney — unhurried village life
Old man's greasy beshmet and nightcap: poverty, comfort, age
Ladder leaning against roof: vertical movement, escape route implied
Sunburnt, shrivelled neck — physical decay contrasting with Hadji Murat's vitality

locations.name: INT. OLD MAN'S SAKLYA / SADO'S SAKLYA - NIGHT
locations.cinematic_description: Dark interior, low ceiling. Warm colour burst as the woman enters — yellow, red, blue against stone grey. Cushions, weapons hung on walls, felt mats, a prayer ritual, whispered intelligence about Shamil's orders. The space contracts around Hadji Murat. Multiple characters cycle in — woman, old man, Eldar, Sado, Sado's son, Bata — the room filling with a conspiracy of hospitality.
locations.atmosphere: Brief sanctuary. Domesticity as refuge. The hospitality ritual creates a fragile bubble of safety — the audience knows it cannot last. As Shamil's threat is revealed, the warmth cools to controlled fear.
locations.timeframe: Act 1 — daytime (initial entry) and night (extended council scene), November 1851
locations.narrative_function: Core exposition scene: HM's defection rationale, Shamil's kill order, the plan to contact the Russians. All major Act 1 information passes through this room.
production_notes: Thin, creaking door — sound design: the creak announces every entry
Outer door and inner door structure — staging depth, character blocking
Woman's costume: yellow smock, red beshmet, wide blue trousers — vivid against grey stone
Cushions, felt mats, weapons on wall: domestic detail that doubles as arsenal
Hadji Murat's slight limp — recurring physical motif, established on first crossing of threshold
Both men pray together (palms upward, stroking faces/beards) — liturgical staging moment
Casting note: the old man and Sado are separate characters; old man may be fusable with minor household figure depending on Story Analyst output

timeline.event: Narrator walks through midsummer meadow, gathers nosegay of wildflowers, notices crimson thistle ('Tartar') in a ditch, struggles to pluck it, finally breaks it free — stalk frayed, flower diminished. Reflects on the thistle's tenacity.
timeline.time_label: Midsummer, afternoon. Frame narrative — before the main story begins.

timeline.event: Narrator walks home across vast black-earth ploughed field on dusty path toward a hill. Transition from the living meadow to barren, cultivated land.
timeline.time_label: Midsummer, daytime. Immediately follows thistle encounter.

timeline.event: Narrator crosses the black-earth field again; notices a thistle plant that has survived being crushed by a cartwheel — this triggers his memory of the Hadji Murat episode and initiates the main narrative.

Hadji Murat rides into Makhmet, a hostile Chechen aoul 15 miles from Russian territory. The muezzin's evening chant has just ended. The village is filled with kizyak smoke, livestock sounds, and disputing voices.
Hadji Murat and his single murid (Eldar) ride into the aoul, avoiding the main square by taking a narrow side street toward the second saklya cut into the hillside.
Hadji Murat stops at the second saklya; finds an old man asleep on the roof under a sheepskin; wakes him with his whip handle. Old man recognizes him, exchanges Selaam greeting.
Old man descends the ladder, orders his grandson (boy of ~15) to fetch the boy's father from the mosque.
Hadji Murat enters the saklya. Middle-aged woman comes through inner door with cushions. Hadji Murat crosses threshold, limping.

Old man sends child to mosque to fetch his son. Hadji Murat enters the saklya through creaking outer door. Woman arranges cushions. HM and old man exchange traditional greetings ('May thy coming bring happiness' / 'May thy sons live').
timeline.time_label: Night — arrival under cover of darkness.

timeline.event: Hadji Murat removes burka, rifle, sword — old man hangs weapons on wall beside household weapons. HM adjusts pistol, sits on cushions. Both men pray together (palms upward, stroking faces/beards).
Old man cryptically reports no good news; speaks in parable ('hares and eagles') about Russian oppression; mentions Russians burned hay in Mitchit Aoul.
Eldar (HM's murid) enters the saklya, removes weapons, hangs them beside his leader's. Old man seats him on felt beside Hadji Murat.
Old man introduces household as Sado's. Tells story of two Russian soldiers captured the week before — one killed, one sent to Shamil in Veden.
timeline.time_label: Night — same evening as HM's arrival. Reference event 'week before'.

timeline.event: Sado (master of house) enters with his son. Informs Hadji Murat that Shamil has ordered him seized alive or dead; envoys departed the day before. Warns that people fear disobeying Shamil but pledges HM's safety under his roof.
Hadji Murat requests a guide to escort Eldar with a letter to the Russians. Sado nominates his brother Bata; son is sent to fetch him and returns ~10 minutes later with Bata.
timeline.time_label: Night — present action, ~10 minutes after Sado's account.

timeline.event: Hadji Murat asks Bata directly whether he can conduct Eldar to the Russians. Bata agrees. The defection plan is set in motion.

atmosphere.visual_mood: Chromatic abundance collapsing into monochrome erasure
atmosphere.color_palette: Peak saturation: crimson, white, blue, lilac, yellow → stripped to black earth and dust-white path
atmosphere.camera_language: Handheld slow drift in the meadow; crane/drone for the field's scale
atmosphere.lighting: Golden-hour meadow → harsh midday on the field
atmosphere.emotional_register: Wonder and innocence, then quiet dread. The audience does not yet know what is coming.
atmosphere.thematic_signal: Nature as victim of empire; the thistle as defiance against erasure
atmosphere.transition_to_next: Crushed thistle standing upright → cut to Hadji Murat on horseback

atmosphere.visual_mood: Cold epic grandeur compressing into claustrophobic surveillance
atmosphere.color_palette: Blue-black sky, ochre stone, orange ember-glow, cold grey shadow
atmosphere.camera_language: Wide ridge-shot establishing scale → tight low-angle in the lanes
atmosphere.lighting: Night. No artificial sources except kizyak ember and doorway glow.
atmosphere.emotional_register: Watchful tension. Epic scale (mountain peaks) offset by the intimate paranoia of a hunted man.
atmosphere.thematic_signal: The Chechen world as besieged community; Hadji Murat as both fugitive and legend
atmosphere.transition_to_next: Waking the old man on the roof → descent into the saklya interior

atmosphere.color_palette: Stone grey relieved by bursts of textile colour (yellow, red, blue of the woman's costume); weapons gleaming on the wall
atmosphere.camera_language: Interior close-ups; faces in partial shadow; the door-creak as rhythmic punctuation
atmosphere.lighting: Oil lamp or single practical source; darkness beyond the threshold
atmosphere.emotional_register: Hospitality as courage. The rituals of greeting, prayer, and cushion-laying are acts of defiance against Shamil's order. Tension mounts with each new arrival.
atmosphere.thematic_signal: Kunak (host-guest bond) as the moral counterforce to imperial command; Sado's pledge is the act of a free man
atmosphere.transition_to_next: Bata agrees to guide Eldar to the Russians → the defection begins

production_notes.overall_design_brief: Historical war tragedy. Lawrence of Arabia scale in exterior shots; The English Patient intimacy in interior scenes. No romanticisation of violence. Austerity in production design — practical materials, no digital enhancement of landscapes.

costume.Hadji_Murat: Burka over riding clothes; pistol always retained even when weapons are hung; slight limp — must be consistent from first threshold crossing onward
costume.Chechen_men: Beshmet (quilted coat), papakha (sheepskin cap), weapons as daily dress
costume.Chechen_women: Vivid textile colour against grey stone interiors — yellow smock, red beshmet, wide blue trousers. Color contrast is cinematically intentional.
costume.Old_man: Greasy beshmet, nightcap — poverty and age read immediately

production_design.saklya_interior: Stone and clay, low ceiling, single door with practical creak, weapons wall, felt mats, cushions. No furniture beyond basics. The room is a fortress and a home simultaneously.
production_design.aoul_exterior: Practical stone village on hillside. Narrow lanes, buildings carved into rock face. Practical kizyak smoke (low-hanging, aromatic). Livestock for sound and movement.
production_design.prologue_field: Real black-earth field (chernozem). Thistle must be practical prop — three-branched, one broken, soiled but erect. No CGI.
production_design.flower_meadow: Peak midsummer wildflower diversity — practical field, not studio. Crimson thistle in a ditch isolated from the main meadow.

tier_1_suggestions.Hadji_Murat: Mads Mikkelsen — physical coiled energy, military bearing, capable of tragic silence
tier_1_suggestions.Russian_commander_Vorontsov: Ralph Fiennes — imperial authority, moral complexity, The English Patient register

casting_anchors.note: 30+ named characters in the novel. Story Analyst + Graphify preprocessor will identify fusion candidates before casting decisions. Historical anchors (Shamil, Vorontsov, Tsar Nicholas I) are non-fusable.

production_notes.frame_narrative_decision: DIRECTORIAL CHOICE — not default. The thistle prologue is structurally essential as ironic frame (Tolstoy's authorial voice made visible). Cutting it removes the anti-imperial thesis from Act 1. Recommendation: retain as 3-minute cold open. Final decision at Treatment stage.
production_notes.scene_budget_note: 60 scenes budgeted. Chunks 1–5 account for approximately 6–8 scenes (prologue 2, arrival 2, saklya council 3–4). Compression ratio from remaining ~140 chunks must be approximately 1 scene per 3 chunks on average.

recurring_locations.name: EXT. BLACK-EARTH PLOUGHED FIELD - DAY
recurring_locations.notes: Both frame-narrative appearances. Second visit triggers the main story — the crushed-but-standing thistle. Could be shot as single location with two time passes. Set-planning priority: MEDIUM (exterior, practical field, minimal dressing).

recurring_locations.name: EXT. MAKHMET AOUL - NIGHT
recurring_locations.notes: Arrival establishing shot + implied recurring presence as the aoul is the primary Chechen setting for Act 1. Set-planning priority: HIGH (mountain village exterior, practical animals, smoke effects).

recurring_locations.name: EXT. CHECHEN AOUL SIDE STREET - DAY
recurring_locations.notes: Arrival approach + ongoing movement through the village. Narrow practical set, can double with loc_003 dressing. Set-planning priority: MEDIUM-HIGH.

recurring_locations.name: EXT. OLD MAN'S SAKLYA ROOF - DAY
recurring_locations.notes: Initial encounter with old man + penthouse/ladder scene with grandson. Single practical rooftop set. Set-planning priority: MEDIUM.

recurring_locations.name: INT. OLD MAN'S SAKLYA / SADO'S SAKLYA - NIGHT
recurring_locations.notes: The dominant interior of Chunks 3–5. Highest set-planning priority. All major Act 1 plot information passes through this space. Must accommodate 6+ characters, weapons display, prayer staging, and door-creak sound design. Set-planning priority: CRITICAL.

lines.text: Selaam aleikum, Bata. May thy sons live.
lines.subtext: I am binding you to the code of the hearth before I ask anything of you; what I give you next is not a request.
lines.action_before: Turns from the door at last, where his ear had been; rises a hand's breadth to acknowledge the newcomer without leaving the felt.

lines.text: Ne habar?
lines.subtext: Has Shamil's hand reached the road between this saklya and the Russian line already tonight?
lines.action_before: Lets the greeting settle, eyes holding Bata's without blinking.

lines.text: Canst thou conduct my murid to the Russians?
lines.subtext: I have no time to test thee; answer with thy life, for that is what I am weighing.
lines.action_before: A single nod to Sado — received, accepted — then the gaze swings flat and level to Bata.

lines.text: Tonight. By the upper gully, not the ford.
lines.subtext: The envoys left yesterday; the ford will be watched by morning, and I will not explain who watches it.
lines.action_before: Glances once at the door as a dog barks somewhere in the village, then back.

lines.text: Eldar carries the letter. No other hand touches it.
lines.subtext: If thou art taken, thou art taken alone; the letter dies with him, not with thee.
lines.action_before: Rests his palm flat on the felt beside his knee, near — but not on — the hilt of the kinjal.

lines.text: Eldar. Thou wilt go.
lines.subtext: I am not asking whether thou art ready; I am telling thee because I already know thou art.
lines.action_before: Turns his head a quarter toward the young murid, not his whole body — the honor of a look, not a summons.

lines.text: Sado. Thy house has stood tall tonight.
lines.subtext: I know what thy brother's saddle costs thee, and I will not name the cost aloud in this room.
lines.action_before: Lowers his eyes for the first time in the exchange — a beat of stillness, the only thanks a naib permits himself.

lines.text: Now we must eat, and then the horses.
lines.subtext: Speak of small things for a little while so the walls sound like an ordinary house to any ear outside.
lines.action_before: Tilts his head a fraction toward the door; the wind has shifted, and he listens past the fire before speaking.

chunk_005_tension_0_75.context: A commander under Shamil's death-order compresses grief, gratitude, and strategic haste into ritual greeting and clipped imperatives, dispatching his murid and his host's brother into the night without ever naming the stakes.

lines.text: Sit. My house is your house while the lamp burns.
lines.subtext: Inside these four walls the old code still holds — outside it, nothing does.
lines.action_before: Squats in front of Hadji Murad, palms lifted upward, then strokes his face downward in the prayer-gesture.

lines.text: Shamil's envoys rode out yesterday. The people fear him.
lines.subtext: You have hours, not days. Do not mistake my calm for time.
lines.action_before: Pushes the worn cap back from his forehead; his unshaved crown catches the firelight.

lines.text: In my house, no one shall injure my kunak while I live.
lines.subtext: My roof is a vow; betrayal of you under it would be betrayal of my own blood.
lines.action_before: Lays one palm flat on the felt between them, as though pressing the words into the floor.

lines.text: But the open fields — we must think it over.
lines.subtext: I already know we cannot hold you here past the next sundown; decide quickly.
lines.action_before: Glances once at the door, then back — the glance lasts no longer than a breath.

lines.text: A man's roof is narrow. Beyond it, every gully has its own law.
lines.subtext: My honor reaches as far as the beam above us — past that, only a guide can carry you.
lines.action_before: Draws a short line in the dust of the felt with two fingers, then closes the hand.

lines.text: I will send brother Bata. He knows every gully from here to the Russian line.
lines.subtext: I am putting my own blood on the road with you — not just my word.
lines.action_before: Turns his head toward his son without rising, voice lowered.

lines.text: Go and call Bata.
lines.subtext: The decision is made; I will not take it back, even in my own chest.
lines.action_before: Nods once at the boy by the door.

lines.text: Canst thou ride him by the upper path? The lower one has eyes on it since yesterday.
lines.subtext: Shamil's men are already watching the obvious road; take the harder one or do not go.
lines.action_before: Looks at Bata as he enters, then strokes his face downward a second time — this time the gesture is for his brother, not for God.

lines.text: Brother Bata. This is my kunak. What is done for him is done for my house.
lines.subtext: If you fall with him, you fall as a son of this roof — not as a hireling.
lines.action_before: Places a hand briefly on Bata's shoulder, then lets it fall.

lines.text: The horses should eat now, not at dawn.
lines.subtext: Be ready to leave before first light; I will not speak the hour aloud.
lines.action_before: Rises halfway from the squat, listening past the wall to the wind and the dogs.

chunk_005_tension_0_75.context: Sado converts Kunak-code into action without ever naming the cost — ritualizing his house as sanctuary, confessing with a single clause that the open fields are already lost, and committing his own brother Bata to Hadji Murad's road as the only honest measure of his vow.

lines.text: Yes, agha.
lines.subtext: I have heard the order to ride through country sworn to kill us, and it is already decided in me.
lines.action_before: Uncrosses his legs slowly, eyes fixed on Hadji Murad, one hand settling on the hilt of his dagger.

lines.text: The letter. To the Russians. Yes.
lines.subtext: I bind myself to this letter as oath; it reaches Vorontsov or it does not leave my body.
lines.action_before: Rises to one knee on the felt, shoulders squared, gaze never leaving Hadji Murad's face.

lines.text: I will go tonight.
lines.subtext: I will die before I fail to go; the hour is already chosen.
lines.action_before: Glances once at the door, then back — listening past the wind for horses the way his agha is listening.

lines.text: Bata knows the gullies. It is enough.
lines.subtext: If Sado's brother is blood-bound to this, I do not need to weigh him further — and I am telling you so you need not turn to ask.
lines.action_before: A short, measured nod toward Bata in his yellow Circassian coat, without rising fully.

lines.text: They will not take it from me, agha.
lines.subtext: If they stop me on the road, they will find the letter only after they have killed me.
lines.action_before: Presses his palm flat against the breast of his cherkeska, over the place where the letter will ride.

lines.text: By Allah.
lines.subtext: The oath is sealed; there is nothing more to say in this room.
lines.action_before: Lowers his eyes once, then lifts them — stilled, already gone from the saklya in his mind.

chunk_005_tension_0_75.context: A young murid binding himself in silence to a suicide errand — readiness expressed through compression, the body moving before the tongue, so his leader need not turn to ask.

adapted_scenes.action: A dirt path cuts through a field of rye not yet harvested. Wildflowers crowd the roadside - clover, daisies, cornflowers, all in full summer color. A man's boot stops. A gloved hand parts the tall grass at the lip of a drainage ditch. In the ditch: a single thistle, crimson-headed, dense with thorns, standing upright where everything else has been cut or trampled. The hand - now wrapped in a handkerchief - reaches down and grips the stalk. The plant does not yield. The grip tightens. Fibers snap one by one. When the thistle finally comes free it is frayed, crushed, diminished. The man holds it up against the blue sky. Then lets it fall.

emotional_beats.beat: The thistle is spotted - upright, intact, surrounded by destruction
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Curiosity, then recognition of something rare
emotional_beats.character_feels: Arrested attention - something that refuses to be ordinary

emotional_beats.beat: The struggle to uproot the thistle
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Unease - the violence is disproportionate, almost wrong
emotional_beats.character_feels: Determination curdling into doubt

emotional_beats.beat: The thistle falls - destroyed, diminished, out of place
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Quiet grief, a sense of waste
emotional_beats.character_feels: Regret: the thing was beautiful in its place. Now it is not.

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: Tenacity destroyed by the wrong kind of attention - beauty belonging to resistance, not to collections
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Golden late-morning light, haze on the field. The thistle's crimson stands against the dark earth of the ditch - the only pure color in a muted landscape. The handkerchief wrapped around the hand should be white and monogrammed - civilized fabric against wild plant. When the thistle is held up against the sky its thorns are backlit, still beautiful. When it falls, it lands in dirt, not among flowers.

inner_monologue_converted.original: 'But what energy and tenacity!' - narrator's explicit moral reflection
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: The thistle held upright against sky - thorns still catching light - then released. The image carries the thought without a word.

inner_monologue_converted.original: 'I threw it away feeling sorry to have vainly destroyed a flower that looked beautiful in its proper place.'
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: The thistle falls. The narrator does not retrieve it. He walks on. The camera holds on the thistle in the dirt as footsteps recede.

adapted_scenes.transition: Cut to black on the fallen thistle - then title card: HAJI MURAT - then sound before image: distant hoofbeats and the wind of a different landscape entirely
