adapted_scenes.action: A vast black field, freshly turned, stretches to the horizon in all directions. Not a blade of grass survives. A MAN walks the edge of a dust-filmed road, coat dusted, moving uphill. He slows. At the roadside, a single thistle clings to the soil - three branches, one snapped at the joint, a soiled blackened flower drooping from the break. The other two stems still carry their flowers, muddied but upright. A cartwheel rut scores the earth beside them. The man crouches, studies the plant. One hand lifts toward it, then draws back. He straightens and looks across the black expanse.

emotional_beats.beat: Man encounters the thistle against a field of obliteration
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Unease at total erasure of life; then surprise at the single survivor
emotional_beats.character_feels: First contempt for the ruin, then arrested by the plant's tenacity

emotional_beats.beat: Memory ignites
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Transition from pastoral observation to war - a door swings open
emotional_beats.character_feels: Recognition: the plant recalls something he has tried not to remember

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: Tenacity against obliteration; the frame that summons the story
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Palette: raw umber earth, grey-white sky, one bruised purple-black thistle head. The broken stem and the erect stem must both be clearly legible - a visual binary: surrender and refusal.

inner_monologue_converted.original: "What vitality! Man has conquered everything and destroyed millions of plants, yet this one won't submit."
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: The narrator's hand reaches for the plant, stops short. He straightens. Looks across the field. Then back at the thistle.

inner_monologue_converted.original: "How many different plant-lives he destroys to support his own existence!"
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Camera registers the absolute blankness of the field. The scale of the destruction is the argument.

adapted_scenes.transition: The man stares at the thistle. A sound drifts in - distant, barely audible - a muezzin call. His eyes lose focus. The field dissolves.

adapted_scenes.action: November dusk in the Chechen mountains. The aoul of Makhmet hugs a hillside, its saklyas packed tight as honeycomb cells. Blue-grey kizyak smoke hangs in the cold air. Cattle low and push past one another toward shelter. Women move quickly at the fountain below the village; their voices carry up, then cut off. HADJI MURAD rides in at the head of two murids. He sits the saddle without ease - a slight forward lean, weight favoring the left. His weapons are visible but not displayed. His eyes move across rooflines before they find faces. He does not pause at the village edge. He rides straight in.

emotional_beats.beat: Hadji Murad enters enemy-adjacent territory as if he owns it
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Tension: is this confidence or desperation?
emotional_beats.character_feels: Controlled calculation: every angle assessed

emotional_beats.beat: The village absorbs him - no alarm, no greeting
emotional_beats.audience_feels: His presence is known but not welcomed
emotional_beats.character_feels: They fear Shamil, not him - that is useful

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: A hunted man who still moves like a commander
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Palette shifts from the field's dead umber to smoke-blue and firelit amber inside the village. Hadji Murad's horse is the tallest thing in frame on arrival. The limp, when he dismounts, is the first crack in the commander silhouette.

inner_monologue_converted.original: Hadji Murad was Shamil's naib, famous for his exploits, who used never to ride out without his banner
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: No banner. No escort beyond two men. The absence is information.

adapted_scenes.transition: He hands his reins to ELDAR without looking at him. Eldar takes them without being asked.

adapted_scenes.action: A single room: earthen floor, a fire in the far corner, a carpet spread near the center, a weapon hung on the wall. SADO squats near the fire, poking at the embers with a stick. He does not rise when Hadji Murad enters, but his hand stills on the stick. Hadji Murad stands in the doorway until Sado looks up. Then he crosses the threshold. ELDAR remains at the door, outside. Sado sets the stick down. He rises, touches his face - a prayer-gesture, brief - then spreads one hand toward the carpet.

dialog_draft.subtext: I am placing myself under the protection of your hearth. Betrayal of me now is betrayal of your house.
dialog_draft.action_before: He stops just inside the door. His right hand is visible, away from the sword hilt. He waits.

dialog_draft.line: Aleikum selaam. My house is thy house.
dialog_draft.subtext: I know what you are carrying into this room. I am choosing to take it on.
dialog_draft.action_before: He gestures to the carpet. Not looking at the door - at Hadji Murad's hands.

dialog_draft.subtext: Is Shamil's hand already here? How many hours do I have?
dialog_draft.action_before: He sits on the carpet, cross-legged. His eyes go to the wall - the single window, then the door.

dialog_draft.line: No news. The mountains are quiet tonight.
dialog_draft.subtext: There is news. It is not quiet. I am giving you the gift of an hour before I tell you.
dialog_draft.action_before: He crouches at the fire, adds a dried thorn-branch. The flame takes. He watches it.

emotional_beats.beat: The greeting ritual - each word is a binding contract
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The weight of ancient codes
emotional_beats.character_feels: Hadji Murad: the first real stillness in days. Sado: the full cost of this hospitality, already computing.

emotional_beats.beat: Hadji Murad asks for intelligence, Sado defers
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Something is being withheld
emotional_beats.character_feels: Hadji Murad reads the pause. He knows. He lets Sado have the lie for now.

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: Hospitality as courage; two men speaking around the danger neither yet names
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Firelight only - no other source. The room is very small; two men fill it. Shadows shift as the fire moves. Eldar visible through the doorframe behind - a silhouette, hand on the doorpost, watching the lane.

inner_monologue_converted.original: The village filled with the scented smoke of burning kizyak; the strained chant of the muezzin had just ceased
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: The muezzin's call is audible from outside as Hadji Murad crosses the threshold - it ends as the door closes.

on_the_nose_risk: Do not have Sado say anything like 'you are in danger' - this must remain unspoken
adapted_scenes.transition: Sado's wife appears in the inner doorway - a brief look, then she withdraws. The fire settles. Outside, a dog barks once and stops.

adapted_scenes.slug: EXT. CHECHEN AOUL - NARROW SIDE STREET - DAY
adapted_scenes.action: Two riders move through a dusty side street of a mountain aoul, deliberately skirting the open square. The lead rider is wrapped in a hooded burka that conceals his face; a rifle barrel protrudes below the fabric. He rides slowly, eyes scanning every doorway and rooftop. His companion is younger, powerfully built, equally cloaked. At the second saklya cut into the hillside, the lead rider halts. He studies the rooftop. Behind a clay chimney, a figure lies motionless under a sheepskin. The lead rider reaches out with his leather whip and taps the figure once. A click of tongue.

dialog_draft.subtext: I am placing myself under the protection of your hearth. Betray me and you betray your own house.
dialog_draft.action_before: He pulls back his hood. The old man on the roof blinks his lashless red eyelids open, then breaks into a toothless smile of recognition.

emotional_beats.beat: A man who once commanded dozens now creeps through a back alley with one rider.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The weight of fall - this is a great man in flight.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Controlled vigilance. Every glance is a calculation.

emotional_beats.beat: The old man recognizes him immediately despite the disguise.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Relief, then renewed tension - being known is being vulnerable.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Relief, instantly suppressed. Recognition means exposure.

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: Concealment and the cost of recognition
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Late afternoon amber light on pale clay walls. The burka is dark, shapeless - swallowing identity. The rifle barrel catching light is the only betrayal of the man underneath.

inner_monologue_converted.original: Hadji Murad peering with his quick black eyes into the faces of those he met on his way
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Camera reads his eyes scanning every rooftop and doorway as he deliberately avoids the main road

adapted_scenes.transition: The old man descends the ladder backward, mumbling - the creak of wood carries into the saklya interior

adapted_scenes.action: A fifteen-year-old boy bursts from the saklya door and stops short, staring at Hadji Murad with wide sloe-black eyes. The old man shoves past him and pulls open the creaking door. Eldar has already taken the horses; Hadji Murad walks with a slight limp into the shadowed entrance. Inside: a spare, middle-aged woman in yellow smock and wide blue trousers carries an armful of cushions toward the front wall. She sets them along the wall without meeting his eyes, bending nearly double. Hadji Murad removes his burka, rifle, and sword, hands them to the old man, who hangs them carefully beside the house weapons. Hadji Murad settles onto the cushions. The old man squats beside him. Both close their eyes and raise their palms upward.

dialog_draft.subtext: You are welcome here. Whatever you carry with you stays outside that door.
dialog_draft.action_before: She sets down the last cushion, then straightens and turns her face to the wall as she speaks - a woman does not look directly at an armed guest.

dialog_draft.subtext: I receive your protection and I bind myself to your house by accepting it.
dialog_draft.action_before: He hands the rifle to the old man without looking at either of them.

dialog_draft.subtext: Say the thing you came to ask me. I will not make it easy for you.
dialog_draft.action_before: After the prayer, both men stroke their beards downward in unison.

dialog_draft.line: Habar yok. I live at the apiary. My son knows.
dialog_draft.subtext: I will not be the one to tell you what you fear. Go to Sado.
dialog_draft.action_before: He sits with his eyes cast down, hands settled on his bare heels.

emotional_beats.beat: Hadji Murad surrenders his weapons - the first act of trust, or submission, he has performed in years.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Vulnerability. This man who commands by silence is now dependent on a stranger's wall.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Controlled exposure. Every object removed is a calculation.

emotional_beats.beat: The shared prayer. Both men raise their palms in the same gesture.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: A brief stillness - the only moment where Hadji Murad is not calculating.
emotional_beats.character_feels: A breath. Then the calculation resumes.

emotional_beats.beat: The old man will not say what he knows. Hadji Murad does not press.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Tension through restraint - the unsaid is louder than any accusation.
emotional_beats.character_feels: He already knows the answer.

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: Refuge accepted under the shadow of what is not yet said
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Interior: clean whitewashed clay walls, the two gleaming copper basins, weapons hung between them like icons. Afternoon light through a small window.

inner_monologue_converted.original: Hadji Murad understood that the old man did not wish to say what he knew
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Hadji Murad nods once and does not ask again - the decision made visible through stillness

inner_monologue_converted.original: The old man looking not at Hadji Murad's face but at his breast
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Played physically: the old man's gaze directed at Hadji Murad's chest throughout - avoidance as language

on_the_nose_risk: Ensure the wife's hospitality greeting does not become warm - keep it ritual, formal, eyes averted
adapted_scenes.transition: The door opens: Sado enters, filling the frame - a man who already knows what the visit means

adapted_scenes.slug: INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - OUTER ROOM - NIGHT
adapted_scenes.action: The old man - the apiarist - swings open a thin, creaking door and presses himself against the frame as Hadji Murad enters. A woman in a yellow smock steps through the inner doorway carrying cushions, her back bending low as she arranges them against the wall without meeting his eyes. Hadji Murad removes his burka, rifle, and sword in one practiced sequence and passes them to the old man. The old man hangs them carefully beside the master's weapons on nails between two large gleaming basins - the only bright objects in the whitewashed room. Hadji Murad adjusts the pistol at the small of his back, crosses to the cushions, and sits.

dialog_draft.subtext: A ritual welcome that also signals: no danger has arrived before you.
dialog_draft.action_before: She bends nearly double arranging the last cushion, face averted, speaking to the floor.

dialog_draft.subtext: I accept your protection. Your household is now bound to mine.
dialog_draft.action_before: He hands the old man his rifle without looking at him, eyes moving to the walls, the door, the window slit.

emotional_beats.beat: Hadji Murad enters a space that is not his - and immediately reads it for exits and threat.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Tension beneath the courtesy: is this house safe, or a trap with hospitality as cover?
emotional_beats.character_feels: Controlled vigilance displaced into ritual precision - every gesture of welcome he performs is also a security assessment.

emotional_beats.beat: The weapons are hung beside the master's - equalizing the guest and the host in custom.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The weight of the old code: under this roof, the old rules still hold.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Momentary relief. The code is intact here.

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: Refuge purchased through ritual - but both host and guest know the price of shelter is rising.
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Clay-plastered white walls, two polished basins reflecting firelight - the only luxury. Weapons arrayed between them like a small arsenal. The woman's yellow smock is the only warm color in the room.

inner_monologue_converted.original: Hadji Murad understood that the old man did not wish to say what he knew
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Hadji Murad's eyes track from the old man's face to his chest - a beat - then he nods once and asks nothing more.

adapted_scenes.transition: The old man squats opposite Hadji Murad. Both lift their palms. The prayer begins in the same instant - as if rehearsed for years.

adapted_scenes.slug: INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - OUTER ROOM - CONTINUOUS
adapted_scenes.action: Hadji Murad and the old man sit cross-legged on the cushions, facing each other. Both raise their palms upward, murmur a prayer, then draw their hands down their beards in unison. The room is silent except for the fire settling. The old man's eyes - red-rimmed, lifeless - rest on Hadji Murad's chest, never his face. Eldar enters from outside, moves across the earthen floor without sound despite his size. He removes his weapons in the same order as his leader and hangs them on the same nails. He settles onto a piece of felt and crosses his legs. His eyes go to the door.

dialog_draft.subtext: Is Shamil's hand already reaching here? Are we safe tonight?
dialog_draft.action_before: After the prayer. He lowers his hands to his knees. His gaze finally meets the old man's.

dialog_draft.line: Habar yok. I live at the apiary. I came only today to see my son. He knows.
dialog_draft.subtext: I know what you need to hear but I will not say it aloud in this room.
dialog_draft.action_before: He keeps his eyes on Hadji Murad's chest, not his face. A pause long enough to be refusal.

dialog_draft.subtext: I have heard the refusal. I will not press a host who signals danger.
dialog_draft.action_before: He nods once - a slight downward tilt of the head. Says nothing.

dialog_draft.line: There is no good news. The hares keep discussing how to drive away the eagles. The eagles tear one and then another. The other day the Russian dogs burnt the hay in the Mitchit aoul.
dialog_draft.subtext: We are losing. The villages are burning. Your people cannot protect you and we cannot protect ourselves.
dialog_draft.action_before: He shifts his weight - the first movement since sitting. His voice drops, goes hoarse.

dialog_draft.subtext: This is the only prayer I have left.
dialog_draft.action_before: He says it without heat, the way a man recites a fact he has accepted.

dialog_draft.subtext: How many men are sheltering in my son's house tonight?
dialog_draft.action_before: He turns his head toward Eldar without raising his eyes past the young man's waist.

dialog_draft.line: My murid. Eldar is his name.
dialog_draft.subtext: He is bound to me. What you give me, you give him.
dialog_draft.action_before: He does not look at Eldar. States it the way he would state a direction of travel.

dialog_draft.subtext: Two men I can account for. Two mouths. Two swords. Still manageable.
dialog_draft.action_before: He nods toward a piece of felt on the floor beside Hadji Murad. Eldar is already moving before the gesture completes.

emotional_beats.beat: The greeting-question Ne habar / Habar yok: a ritual exchange that carries everything the speakers dare not say aloud.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Something is very wrong - worse than the old man will name.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Hadji Murad measures the silence.

emotional_beats.beat: The hares-and-eagles speech - a folk parable delivered as cold fact.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The countryside is being destroyed.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Old man: a grief too large for anger. Hadji Murad: still, receiving this as tactical intelligence.

emotional_beats.beat: Eldar takes his place on the felt without being invited - the single downward nod suffices.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The wordless bond between Hadji Murad and his murid.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Eldar: present, alert, silent. An extension of his leader's will.

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: Intelligence delivered in code - what is not said is more dangerous than what is.
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Fire dying low. The old man's red-rimmed eyes catch light but give nothing back. Eldar enters like a shadow gaining weight - his stillness on the felt is total, watchful.

inner_monologue_converted.original: Hadji Murad, understanding that the old man did not wish to say what he knew
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Hadji Murad holds the old man's non-gaze for three seconds, then gives one nod - the kind that closes a door.

on_the_nose_risk: The hares-and-eagles speech is already subtext - resist any urge to paraphrase
adapted_scenes.transition: Eldar's eyes fix on the door. A sound from outside - distant, unclear. His hand does not move to his dagger. Not yet.

adapted_scenes.action: An oil wick burns low on a clay shelf. The old apiarist sits cross-legged on a worn felt rug, stacking walnut shells into a small pile as he speaks. Hadji Murad sits opposite him, back straight against the mud wall, eyes fixed on the plank door. Eldar enters behind him in silence, lowers himself onto a strip of felt, crosses his legs, and places both hands flat on his thighs. Outside, a dog barks twice and stops. Hadji Murad's head turns a fraction toward the sound, then back.

dialog_draft.subtext: Name him so my roof knows whom it shelters.
dialog_draft.action_before: Sets a walnut shell down on the pile without looking up.

dialog_draft.line: My murid. Eldar is his name.
dialog_draft.subtext: He is mine; what falls on him falls on me.
dialog_draft.action_before: Places his right hand flat over his heart, one beat, then lowers it.

dialog_draft.subtext: Your word is enough; sit and be at peace under this roof.
dialog_draft.action_before: Nods once and motions with an open palm toward the felt beside Hadji Murad.

dialog_draft.line: Last week our fellows took two Russian soldiers on the Argun road. One they killed. The other they sent to Shamil in Veden.
dialog_draft.subtext: Shamil's reach is long, and I am telling you so.
dialog_draft.action_before: Pushes the small pile of walnut shells toward Eldar in offer.

dialog_draft.subtext: I hear. I do not speak until I am asked.
dialog_draft.action_before: Fixes his ram-like eyes on the old man. Hand rests quiet on the dagger hilt at his belt.

dialog_draft.subtext: I am listening for horses; keep talking so the room sounds normal.
dialog_draft.action_before: Tilts his head a degree toward the door. A horse snorts softly in the yard. He closes his eyes for a breath, opens them.

emotional_beats.beat: A guest and his murid are seated under the roof of a stranger - hospitality opened, not yet sealed.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The quiet before a hinge turns.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Hadji Murad: tight vigilance masked as stillness. Eldar: weight of being the one hand between his lord and the door.

emotional_beats.beat: The old man's news about the Russians on the Argun road lands as a marker - the outside world is already knocking.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The walls of the saklya are thinner than they look.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Hadji Murad: private arithmetic; Shamil's hand is closer than yesterday.

adapted_scenes.visual_note: Single oil wick, amber pooling on clay walls and felt rugs; shadows long and low. Props: walnut shells, wooden bowl, plank door, dagger hilt at Eldar's belt.

inner_monologue_converted.original: Hadji Murad heard him absently, looking at the door and listening to the sounds outside.
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Head turning a fraction toward a dog's bark; eyes on the door while hand rests on sword hilt; brief closed-eye breath at a horse's snort.

adapted_scenes.transition: The creak of the door from outside - wooden slippers being shed - carries into the next scene as Sado enters.

adapted_scenes.action: Footsteps under the penthouse. The plank door creaks open. Sado enters first - forty, long-nosed, small-bearded - his fifteen-year-old son slipping in behind him and dropping onto his heels by the door. Sado steps out of his wooden slippers, pushes his worn cap back on an unshaved head, and squats in front of Hadji Murad without a word. He lifts his palms, closes his eyes, murmurs a prayer. Then he strokes his face downward, slowly, with both hands. Only then does he open his mouth.

dialog_draft.line: An order came. From Shamil. To take thee alive, or dead.
dialog_draft.subtext: I would not speak these words if I had any softer way to warn thee.
dialog_draft.action_before: Opens his eyes on Hadji Murad and does not blink. One hand settles flat on his own knee.

dialog_draft.line: His envoys were in this aul yesterday. They left at first light.
dialog_draft.subtext: The hunt has begun. The clock is not days. It is hours.
dialog_draft.action_before: Tilts his chin toward the door, toward the direction the envoys rode.

dialog_draft.line: The people are afraid to disobey. We must be careful.
dialog_draft.subtext: Do not trust the faces of this village past tomorrow.
dialog_draft.action_before: Glances once at his son by the door. The boy lowers his eyes.

dialog_draft.line: In my house, no one shall injure my kunak while I live.
dialog_draft.subtext: My roof is the last roof that still binds the old code for you.
dialog_draft.action_before: Touches the felt rug between them - a small pressing-down with the flat of his palm, as if sealing it.

dialog_draft.line: But in the open fields?...We must think it over.
dialog_draft.subtext: Beyond this door I cannot save thee; decide now, or too late.
dialog_draft.action_before: The palm lifts from the rug. His eyes hold Hadji Murad's. A long pause; the oil wick gutters.

dialog_draft.subtext: I have heard what thou refused to say. I will not force thee to say it.
dialog_draft.action_before: Nods once, slow. Eyes move to the door, then back.

dialog_draft.subtext: The decision is already made; we only need the means.
dialog_draft.action_before: Lays his right hand on the sword hilt at his hip, then releases it.

dialog_draft.line: Now we must send a man with a letter to the Russians. My murid will go. He will need a guide.
dialog_draft.subtext: I am choosing the lesser captor; do not make me explain why.
dialog_draft.action_before: Turns his head a quarter-degree toward Eldar - no look, only orientation.

emotional_beats.beat: Sado performs the prayer-gesture before speaking - the ritual weight that binds what follows.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: A sacred register; this is not gossip, this is an oath-message.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Sado: the grave duty of telling a kunak he is hunted.

emotional_beats.beat: Sado's antithesis - inside the house vs. the open fields - lays bare the limit of what hospitality can hold.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The walls shrink around both men.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Sado: alarm displaced into code. Hadji Murad: the last door to Shamil closes.

emotional_beats.beat: Hadji Murad's single nod and 'Now we must -' - the pivot from listening to decision.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: A threshold crossed without ceremony.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Hadji Murad: cold clarity; Eldar: sudden awareness he is the instrument.

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: A man tells a guest he is being hunted, and the guest decides to cross over to the other side.
adapted_scenes.visual_note: Sado's worn cap pushed back off an unshaved crown; wooden slippers by the door; the oil wick's flame flickering when the door was opened.

inner_monologue_converted.original: He too lifted his palms upwards, as the old man had done, repeated a prayer, and then stroked his face downwards. Only after that did he begin to speak.
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Full prayer-gesture performed on screen as the scene's preamble - Sado's grave cadence is established by ritual before a word of news is spoken.

inner_monologue_converted.original: Hadji Murad listened with attention and nodded approvingly.
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Single slow nod; hand to sword hilt and released; no verbal acknowledgement before the pivot line 'Now we must -'.

adapted_scenes.transition: Sado turns to the boy; the boy's bare feet strike the packed earth as he bolts - footfalls carry into the next scene.

adapted_scenes.slug: INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - LATER THAT NIGHT
adapted_scenes.action: The door stands half open; cold night air moves the flame. Sado's son is gone. Hadji Murad has not risen. Eldar checks his dagger - slides it a finger's width from the sheath, seats it again. Sado prepares provisions: wraps hard cheese and flat bread in a square of oiled cloth, sets it beside Eldar without speaking. Ten minutes pass in near silence. Footsteps outside - light, then heavier. The boy returns, Bata behind him: short-legged, sinewy, sun-black skin, yellow Circassian coat frayed at the sleeves.

dialog_draft.subtext: I am committing my own blood to thy survival, not only my word.
dialog_draft.action_before: Stands from the squat, presses a hand once to his own chest, then lets it fall.

dialog_draft.subtext: I place this exchange under the old greeting; what follows is binding.
dialog_draft.action_before: Rises. Right hand over heart. Holds Bata's eyes.

dialog_draft.subtext: I receive thee as guest and speaker.
dialog_draft.action_before: Touches cap to chest, then ducks his head once.

dialog_draft.line: Canst thou conduct my murid to the Russians?
dialog_draft.subtext: I have no allies left; I am choosing the lesser captor - say yes or no, now.
dialog_draft.action_before: No preamble, no explanation. Eyes steady on Bata's face.

dialog_draft.line: I can. By the mountain paths above the river. Before dawn.
dialog_draft.subtext: I know the road and I know what it will cost if we are seen.
dialog_draft.action_before: Grins - a flash of white against sun-blackened skin. Slaps his own thigh once.

dialog_draft.line: Eldar. The letter. Thou goest with him.
dialog_draft.subtext: Thy life is this paper; do not return without an answer.
dialog_draft.action_before: Draws a small folded paper from inside his beshmet. Holds it out on a flat palm.

dialog_draft.subtext: I bind myself to this order as oath. I will die before I fail to carry it.
dialog_draft.action_before: Rises in one motion. Takes the paper with both hands. Tucks it inside his coat against the skin.

dialog_draft.subtext: I will die before I fail to go.
dialog_draft.action_before: One short nod to Hadji Murad. Hand settles on the dagger hilt.

dialog_draft.subtext: The matter is now in God's hands and the horses'.
dialog_draft.action_before: Bows his head. Palms upward a breath, then down over his face.

emotional_beats.beat: Sado offers Bata - his own brother - as guide. The offer of blood.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Gravity; the host has just doubled the stake.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Sado: duty accepted as cost. Hadji Murad: recognition that the debt has grown.

emotional_beats.beat: Hadji Murad's direct question to Bata - no preamble - and Bata's light, grinning 'I can.'
emotional_beats.audience_feels: Relief puncturing the weight; a spark of plan.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Hadji Murad: a small release in the shoulders. Bata: pride of being chosen.

emotional_beats.beat: Eldar takes the letter with both hands, against the skin under his coat. The defection is now a physical object on a body moving out the door.
emotional_beats.audience_feels: The hinge of Act 1 has turned.
emotional_beats.character_feels: Eldar: an oath sealed in silence. Hadji Murad: the lesser captor is chosen.

adapted_scenes.emotional_core: The moment of commitment - a letter leaves the hand of the naib and begins to cross.
adapted_scenes.visual_note: The folded paper - small, cream-coloured, creased along one edge - passed from palm to palm. Bata's yellow coat catching the lamp.

inner_monologue_converted.original: The boy instantly bounded to his nimble feet as if he were on springs, and swinging his arms, rapidly left the saklya.
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: Silent springing exit; urgency shown in body language only, no line needed.

inner_monologue_converted.original: Hadji Murad is preparing to defect to the Russians (internal decision).
inner_monologue_converted.converted_to: The folded letter drawn from inside his beshmet and laid on Eldar's palm - the decision is now a physical object in motion.

adapted_scenes.transition: The folded letter disappearing under Eldar's coat, against his skin - the object carries into the next sequence as Eldar and Bata ride out.

final_script:
HADJI MURAT

SCREENPLAY

Based on the literary work by Lev Tolstoi
Adapted by AI Adaptation Pipeline
────────────────────────────────────────

EXT. RUSSIAN COUNTRYSIDE - DAY (MIDSUMMER)

A dirt path cuts through rye not yet harvested. Wildflowers crowd the roadside — clover, daisies, cornflowers, all in full summer color.

A man's boot stops at the lip of a drainage ditch.

In the ditch: a single thistle, crimson-headed, dense with thorns, upright where everything around it has been cut or trampled.

A gloved hand, wrapped in a white monogrammed handkerchief, reaches down and grips the stalk. The plant does not yield. The grip tightens. Fibers snap one by one. When the thistle comes free it is frayed, crushed, diminished. The hand holds it against the blue sky — thorns backlit, still beautiful.

Then lets it fall. It lands in dirt.

CUT TO BLACK.

TITLE CARD: HAJI MURAT

The sound arrives before any image: distant hoofbeats, and the wind of another landscape entirely.


EXT. PLOUGHED FIELD - LATE AFTERNOON

A vast black field, freshly turned, stretches to the horizon. Not a blade of grass survives.

A MAN walks the edge of a dust-filmed road, coat grey with road-dust, moving uphill. He slows.

At the roadside: a single thistle, three branches, one snapped at the joint. A soiled blackened flower droops from the break. The other two stems still carry their flowers — muddied, upright. A cartwheel rut scores the earth beside them.

The man crouches. Studies the plant. One hand lifts toward it — then draws back.

He straightens. Looks across the black expanse.

The field dissolves. A muezzin call drifts in, barely audible — then grows.


EXT. MAKHMET VILLAGE - DUSK

November in the Chechen mountains. The aoul of Makhmet clings to a hillside, its saklyas packed tight as honeycomb. Blue-grey kizyak smoke hangs low in the cold air.

Cattle push past one another toward shelter. Women move quickly at the fountain below, their voices carrying up — then cutting off.

HADJI MURAD rides in at the head of two murids — a lean, watchful man who sits the saddle with a slight forward lean, weight favoring the left. His weapons are visible but not displayed.

His eyes cross every roofline before they find a face. He does not pause at the village edge. He rides straight in.

He hands his reins to ELDAR — broad-shouldered, black-eyed, young — without looking at him.

Eldar takes them without being asked.


INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - NIGHT

A single room. Earthen floor. A fire in the far corner. A carpet at the center. Weapons hung on the wall.

SADO squats near the fire, poking at embers with a stick. He does not rise when Hadji Murad enters — but his hand stills on the stick.

Hadji Murad stands in the doorway until Sado looks up. Then he crosses the threshold.

Eldar remains outside the door. A silhouette in the frame, one hand on the doorpost, watching the lane.

Sado sets the stick down. He rises. Touches his face — a brief prayer-gesture — then spreads one hand toward the carpet.

HADJI MURAD
Selaam aleikum.

SADO
Aleikum selaam. My house is thy house.

Hadji Murad settles onto the carpet. A pause. His eyes move to the door, then back.

HADJI MURAD
Ne habar?

Sado does not answer immediately. The fire settles.

SADO
No news. The mountains are quiet tonight.

A beat. Sado's wife appears in the inner doorway — a brief look, then she withdraws. Outside, a dog barks once and stops.


EXT. CHECHEN AOUL - NARROW SIDE STREET - DAY

Two riders move through a dusty side street, deliberately skirting the open square.

The lead rider is wrapped in a hooded burka that swallows his face. A rifle barrel protrudes below the fabric. He rides slowly, scanning every doorway and rooftop.

His companion — ELDAR — is younger, powerfully built, equally cloaked.

At the second saklya cut into the hillside, the lead rider halts. He studies the rooftop.

Behind a clay chimney, a figure lies motionless under a sheepskin.

The rifle barrel — the only thing catching light beneath the burka — tips slightly. A leather whip extends and taps the figure once. A click of tongue.

HADJI MURAD
Selaam aleikum.

The sheepskin stirs.


INT. SAKLYA - SADO'S HOUSE - DAY

A fifteen-year-old boy bursts from the inner doorway and stops dead, staring. SADO'S SON — sloe-black eyes, bare feet, no older than his fear.

The old man shoves past him and hauls open the creaking door.

ELDAR has already taken the horses. HADJI MURAD walks through, a slight limp marking every third step into the shadowed entrance.

Inside: spare whitewashed clay, a small window, two gleaming copper basins on the wall catching afternoon light. Between them, weapons hung like icons. A WOMAN in a yellow smock and wide blue trousers carries an armful of cushions to the front wall. She bends nearly double arranging them, eyes never lifting.

Hadji Murad removes his burka, rifle, and sword in a single practiced sequence. He passes them to the old man, who hangs them carefully between the basins — beside the master's weapons, equal in rank.

Hadji Murad adjusts the pistol at the small of his back. He crosses to the cushions and settles.

SADO'S WIFE
May thy coming bring happiness.

HADJI MURAD
May thy sons live.

The old man — THE APIARIST, red-rimmed eyes, skin like old bark — squats beside him. Both men raise their palms upward. Their eyes close.

A long beat. The fire settles. The prayer runs out.

APIARIST
Ne habar?

Hadji Murad's eyes open. He holds the old man's gaze.

HADJI MURAD
Ne habar.

APIARIST
Habar yok. I live at the apiary. I came only today to see my son.

A pause. The old man does not look away.

APIARIST (CONT'D)
He knows.

Hadji Murad nods once. He does not press.

The door opens. SADO enters — filling the frame, already knowing what the visit means.


INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - OUTER ROOM - NIGHT

The old man presses himself against the door frame as Hadji Murad enters. Sado's wife carries cushions from the inner room, back curved low, arranging them along the wall without a word.

Hadji Murad removes his burka, rifle, and sword and passes them to the old man. The old man hangs them on nails between the two polished basins — the only brightness in the room. The yellow smock moves through the doorway and disappears.

Hadji Murad crosses to the cushions and sits.

SADO'S WIFE
May thy coming bring happiness.

HADJI MURAD
May thy sons live.

The old man squats opposite. Hadji Murad watches the door.

Both men raise their palms in the same instant. The prayer begins. Neither waited for the other.


INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - OUTER ROOM - CONTINUOUS

They sit cross-legged on the cushions, facing each other. Palms lower. Hands draw down beards in unison. The fire settles to coals.

The Apiarist's red-rimmed eyes rest on Hadji Murad's chest. Never his face.

ELDAR enters from outside — large, soundless despite his size. He removes his weapons in the same order as his leader and hangs them on the same nails. He lowers himself onto a strip of felt, crosses his legs, places both hands flat on his thighs.

His eyes go to the door.

HADJI MURAD
Ne habar?

APIARIST
Habar yok. I live at the apiary. I came only today to see my son.

He stops. Lets the silence breathe.

APIARIST (CONT'D)
He knows.

Hadji Murad does not press.

APIARIST (CONT'D)
There is no good news. The hares keep discussing how to drive away the eagles. The eagles tear one and then another. The other day the Russian dogs burnt the hay in the Mitchit aoul.

A beat.

APIARIST (CONT'D)
May their faces be torn.

He looks at Eldar. His gaze is flat, measuring.

APIARIST (CONT'D)
Who is he?

HADJI MURAD
My murid. Eldar is his name.

APIARIST
That is well.

Eldar's eyes stay on the door. A sound from outside — distant, blurred by clay walls. His hand does not move to his dagger.

Not yet.


INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - NIGHT

An oil wick burns low on a clay shelf. Amber pools across felt rugs and mud walls, shadows long and close to the floor.

The Apiarist sits cross-legged on a worn felt rug, stacking walnut shells into a small pile as he speaks. Hadji Murad sits opposite, back straight against the mud wall, eyes fixed on the plank door.

ELDAR enters behind him in silence, lowers himself onto a strip of felt, crosses his legs, places both hands flat on his thighs.

Outside, a dog barks twice and stops.

Hadji Murad's head turns a fraction toward the sound. Then back.

APIARIST
Who is he?

HADJI MURAD
My murid. Eldar is his name.

APIARIST
That is well.

He sets another walnut shell on the pile. Considers it.

APIARIST (CONT'D)
Last week our fellows took two Russian soldiers on the Argun road. One they killed. The other they sent to Shamil in Veden.

Eldar's jaw tightens. He does not speak.

Hadji Murad listens past the old man — past the walls — to whatever is outside.

The wooden slippers on the step carry before the door opens. SADO enters.


INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - CONTINUOUS

Footsteps under the penthouse. The plank door creaks open.

SADO — forty, long-nosed, short-bearded — steps inside, his fifteen-year-old son slipping in behind him and dropping onto his heels by the door.

Sado steps out of his wooden slippers. He pushes his worn cap back on an unshaved head. He squats in front of Hadji Murad without a word.

He lifts his palms. Closes his eyes. His lips move through a prayer. Then both hands draw slowly down his face — a long, deliberate stroke from forehead to chin.

Only then does he open his mouth.

SADO
An order came. From Shamil.

He looks at Hadji Murad directly for the first time.

SADO (CONT'D)
To take thee alive. Or dead.

The oil wick flame steadies. No one moves.

SADO (CONT'D)
His envoys were in this aul yesterday. They left at first light.

SADO (CONT'D)
The people are afraid to disobey. We must be careful.

Hadji Murad holds Sado's gaze. He says nothing.

SADO (CONT'D)
In my house, no one shall injure my kunak while I live.

A pause. Sado glances at the door.

SADO (CONT'D)
But in the open fields?...

He lets it land.

SADO (CONT'D)
We must think it over.

Hadji Murad nods once. A single, slight downward movement.

HADJI MURAD
Very well.

A beat — three seconds of complete stillness.

HADJI MURAD (CONT'D)
Now we must send a man with a letter to the Russians. My murid will go. He will need a guide.

Sado turns to the boy by the door. One look — a word unnecessary. The boy's bare feet strike the packed earth as he bolts into the dark.

His footfalls carry into the silence he leaves behind.


INT. SADO'S SAKLYA - LATER THAT NIGHT

The door stands half open. Cold air moves the flame of the oil wick.

SADO squats near the provisions corner, working in silence — hard cheese and flat bread folded into a square of oiled cloth. He sets the bundle beside ELDAR without looking at him.

Eldar draws his dagger a finger's width from the sheath. Seats it again.

HADJI MURAD has not moved from the cushions. His eyes are on the door.

Ten minutes pass. The flame bends once, steadies.

Light footsteps outside, then heavier ones. SADO'S SON enters first, stepping aside to let BATA through — short-legged, sinewy, skin the color of walnut bark, a yellow Circassian coat frayed at the sleeves and wrists.

SADO
(without rising)
I will send brother Bata.

Hadji Murad turns his head. He looks at Bata.

HADJI MURAD
Selaam aleikum.

BATA
Wa aleikum selaam.

HADJI MURAD
Canst thou conduct my murid to the Russians?

Bata's mouth opens — almost a grin, not quite.

BATA
I can. By the mountain paths above the river. Before dawn.

Hadji Murad reaches inside his coat and produces a folded paper — small, cream-colored, creased along one edge. He holds it without extending it.

HADJI MURAD
Eldar. The letter. Thou goest with him.

Eldar rises in a single motion.

ELDAR
The letter. Yes, agha.

He takes the paper with both hands. Slides it inside his coat, against his skin. His hand stays flat over it a moment.

ELDAR (CONT'D)
I will go.

Sado strokes his face once, downward, with both hands. He does not speak.

The boy's bare feet strike the packed earth as he moves to hold the door.

Bata pulls his coat straight at the collar. Eldar checks the door frame — both sides — then steps through.

The flame bends in the draft.

                                                      CUT TO:

EXT. MOUNTAIN PATH - BEFORE DAWN

Darkness. Hoofbeats on stone, moving fast.

coverage_report.synopsis: In midsummer Russian countryside, an unnamed narrator crouches before a crushed thistle in a drainage ditch. November, Chechen mountains. Hadji Murad rides into Makhmet with two murids, takes refuge at Sado's saklya. The Apiarist delivers intelligence in fable-register; Sado prays before reporting Shamil's kill-order. Hadji Murad pivots in a single beat: dispatch Eldar with a letter to the Russians, Bata as guide. Flame bends in draft. Cut to hoofbeats in darkness.

concept.notes: Premise strong — commander between Shamil and Russians is classic double-bind. Thistle frame is powerful. Logline field empty.

coverage_report.overall_comments: Fragment has the bones of a serious film. Visual language disciplined, ritual dialogue system carries cultural subtext, inciting incident lands cleanly. Currently unreadable as professional submission for ONE reason: chunk-overlap deduplication failure. Pushkin attribution compounds this. Both are pipeline failures, not craft failures, but a reader cannot know that. Craft-level fixes are limited: cut one thistle scene, delete subtext captions. Underlying material has genuine festival potential in Lawrence / Come and See / English Patient tier.

fusion_candidates.reason: Both threads are present in this chunk and inseparable: the botanical walk IS the framing device for the thistle. In the film they constitute a single pre-title visual prologue. Confirmed as fusable per Plotline Graph fusion suggestion.

Bata's sole function is to guide the murids to the Russian lines; Khan Mahoma's sole function is to be guided there. In a film these two can be collapsed into one character — a murid-envoy who both knows the terrain and carries the message — eliminating one named walk-on.

The old man delivers the exposition (Shamil's order, burned Mitchit aoul) and then Sado repeats and expands it. In a compressed screenplay Sado alone can carry all this information; the old man is a relay character whose lines fold into the host's opening speech.

Both women appear only to serve food and embody Chechen domestic space. They share no dialogue and can be merged into a single silent female figure without narrative loss. Pre-identified fusion candidate confirmed.

All three are pure atmosphere/hospitality functions in this safe-house scene with no individual arc or name. In a film, the household can be represented by a single figure (e.g., a trusted elder or the woman alone) who wakes, recognises, and welcomes Hadji Murad. Fusing them loses nothing dramaturgically.

The 'strong active murid' who dismounts before the old man in Chunk 3 is the same figure now named Eldar. No fusion needed — they are the same character. Confirm: Eldar = the single murid on the covert ride. His naming here resolves the anonymity from Chunk 3.

Graph pre-identified [0.90]: both are nameless-crowd Russian soldiers in Panov's ambush party with single incidental function. Not present in this chunk but confirmed fusable from graph hint — retain flag.
Graph pre-identified [0.85]: both serve as Avdeev's comrades at the deathbed with nearly interchangeable functions. Not present in this chunk but confirmed fusable from graph hint — retain flag.

Confirmed by chunk: both are minor crowd-soldiers in the ambush party with near-identical functions (sit, react, comment). Nikitin's grumbling about the cashbox and Bondarenko's cheerful Ukrainian voice are interchangeable — either can absorb the other. The cashbox subplot is cuttable; if cut, Nikitin has zero remaining function and can be folded into Bondarenko or dropped entirely. Confidence 0.90 confirmed.

Panov confirmed as present at Avdeev's wounding. Serogin (Avdeev's other deathbed companion from later chapters) shares identical function of loyal witness. Can be merged in film into a single soldier-friend character. Confidence 0.85 confirmed.

Vovilo appears in this chunk as Poltoratsky's servant — passive, reactive. Ivan Petrovich exists as off-screen reference point for Vovilo's self-pity (later chapter). Both are non-load-bearing. If Poltoratsky's domestic scenes are retained at all, one name suffices. Confidence 0.85 confirmed.

Both appear solely as Poltoratsky's companions in the wood-felling breakfast and bedroom scenes respectively. Neither carries independent narrative weight. In compression, Poltoratsky can speak to one generic comrade. Non-historical, low weight.

cuttable.element: Extended flower catalogue (clover, ox-eye daisies, rape blossoms, campanulas, vetch, scabious, plaintains, cornflowers, dodder)
cuttable.reason: Seven-flower enumeration is literary texture, not dramaturgical content. Film can render the meadow visually in 5 seconds. The list is non-load-bearing and cannot be filmed as written without becoming a nature documentary.

cuttable.element: Author-narrator identity as literary device
cuttable.reason: The first-person Tolstoy-as-narrator frame is a prose convention with no direct film equivalent. The film can retain the thistle imagery as a cold-open visual metaphor without an authorial voice-over, or compress to a brief V.O. title card. The narrator as character has zero dramatic weight beyond this prologue.

cuttable.element: Black-earth ploughed field transition
cuttable.reason: Purely transitional description; adds no plot, character, or conflict. Can be cut entirely or absorbed into a landscape cut leading to the main narrative.

cuttable.element: Author-Narrator's thistle monologue (inner thought about man's destructiveness)
cuttable.reason: The interior philosophical reflection ('How many different plant-lives...') is a classic Tolstoyan authorial digression. The film equivalent is a pure visual: close-up of the crushed, upright thistle — no voice-over needed. The reflex to Hadji Murad can be established by cutting directly from the thistle to his face. The verbal meditation is non-load-bearing.

cuttable.element: Mitchit Aoul burnt hay reference
cuttable.reason: Background world-building delivered by the old man. The fact of Russian raids is already established by other means (the defection context). This line is cuttable without information loss.

cuttable.element: Old Man's ritualistic descent from roof (extended physical business)
cuttable.reason: Detailed novelistic staging (blinked eyes, wooden-heeled slippers, shrivelled neck, mumbling) is literary texture, not dramaturgical necessity. A film can show him in two shots.

cuttable.element: Bata's self-promotional speech ('There is not another Chechen who would pass as I can...')
cuttable.reason: Comic character texture. Functional information (he can guide) lands in one line. The boast is cuttable without plot loss.

cuttable.element: Sado's Son as named recurring presence
cuttable.reason: The boy appears three times (running, returning with Bata, sitting by door). He is mood and colour, not plot. Can be cut or reduced to one establishing beat.

cuttable.element: Old man descending the ladder — detailed description of dressing, mumbling, shaking head
cuttable.reason: Tolstoyan naturalistic detail (the toothless mumbling, shrivelled neck, wooden-heeled slippers) is non-load-bearing for the plot. The act of recognition and welcome is what matters; the visual comedy of the slow descent can be compressed to a cut.

cuttable.element: Boy with sloe-black eyes sent to fetch father
cuttable.reason: The boy is a single-function messenger. His physical description and the errand are minor texture. In a compressed film, the father/host can simply already be present or arrive without the relay.

cuttable.element: Narrator's retrospective contrast of Hadji Murad's former retinue (dozens of murids, banner) vs. current state
cuttable.reason: This is an authorial digression summarising past grandeur. In film, the contrast must be shown visually (e.g., a brief flashback or an establishing scene earlier in the film) rather than narrated. The paragraph itself is cuttable as voiceover; its content should be embedded in staging.

cuttable.element: Old Man's Wife carrying cushions and exchanging ritual greeting
cuttable.reason: Purely atmospheric — she has no name, no further function, and the hospitality ritual can be conveyed through the old man alone or through a single visual of prepared cushions. Her lines are filler in a compressed screenplay.

cuttable.element: Mitchit Aoul hay-burning reference
cuttable.reason: Non-load-bearing plot detail. The Russian aggression theme is already established; this specific place-name adds texture but no new narrative information. In compression it becomes a generic 'they burned the village' gesture or is cut entirely.

cuttable.element: Extended description of prayer ritual (eye-closing, palms upward, stroking faces)
cuttable.reason: Visually valuable but over-described for the page. In a screenplay, 'They pray' or a single physical gesture communicates this — the full Tolstoyan description is literary, not cinematic shorthand.

cuttable.element: Company cashbox subplot (commander borrowing from funds)
cuttable.reason: Soldiers' conversation about the missing cashbox money is entirely self-contained and non-load-bearing. It characterizes Nikitin's moroseness but adds nothing to the HM trajectory or Avdeev's arc. Confirmed cuttable thread.

cuttable.element: Baron Freze character arc
cuttable.reason: Freze appears only in the wood-felling officers' breakfast. Reduced to ranks for duelling — interesting detail, zero narrative function. Cut.

cuttable.element: Tikhonov bedroom scene with Poltoratsky
cuttable.reason: Post-card-game scene between Poltoratsky and Tikhonov is pure filler — characterization that can be achieved through Poltoratsky's visible infatuation during the card game itself.

cuttable.element: Vovilo the serf (Poltoratsky's servant)
cuttable.reason: Confirmed cuttable. His resentment/self-pity arc (comparing himself to Ivan Petrovich) is non-load-bearing. His domestic function can be absorbed silently.

cuttable.element: Marya Vasilevna / Poltoratsky infatuation subplot
cuttable.reason: Poltoratsky's romantic distraction during the card game and his reverie afterward are atmospheric but not structurally necessary. Marya Vasilevna's role can be retained minimally (the drawing-room reception of HM) without the infatuation frame.

cuttable.element: General Sleptsov heroism-myth discussion among officers
cuttable.reason: Tolstoy's ironic anti-heroism commentary via the Sleptsov anecdote is a literary digression. Strong thematic content but delivered as authorial voice, not through action. Film can imply the theme through Avdeev's wounding without the officers' meta-commentary.

cuttable.element: Vorontsov's jurisdictional anxiety about Meller-Zakomelsky
cuttable.reason: Confirmed as a cuttable political-framing thread (reach=1). In film, Vorontsov's ambition can be shown through his eagerness to host HM without the bureaucratic sub-anxiety about chain of command.

cuttable.element: Regimental Adjutant and Tutor as card-game characters
cuttable.reason: Both exist solely to populate the card table. Non-historical, zero independent function. The drawing room scene needs only Vorontsov, Marya Vasilevna, and Poltoratsky.

refocalize.scene: Thistle prologue — narrator plucks and destroys the Tartar thistle
refocalize.recommended_pov: Cut narrator entirely. Open on the thistle itself — ECU of crimson flower in a ditch, bumble-bee inside, then a hand (unseen, belonging to no one) tears it free. The internal reflection ('What determination — how dearly it sold its life') becomes a sparse V.O. line or title card. Alternatively: restage as a visual rhyme — cut from destroyed thistle to Hadji Murad's face in the closing chapter, establishing the bookend without an authorial proxy.

refocalize.scene: Thistle prologue — narrator walks the ploughed field, discovers the thistle, reflects philosophically
refocalize.recommended_pov: Remove POV entirely — open on EXTREME CLOSE-UP of the broken-but-upright thistle, then pull back to reveal the empty black field. No narrator. The image is self-sufficient. Cut to: Hadji Murad's face in motion.

refocalize.scene: Old man's arrival — descends from roof, tells news about Shamil's order in proverb-form
refocalize.current_pov: Close third-person following the old man's physical movements
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's POV — he watches and reads the old man. The old man's hesitation and oblique speech register as threat-assessment from HM's perspective rather than as novelistic character sketch.

refocalize.scene: Women serve food — Sado's wife and daughter move through the room, men go silent
refocalize.current_pov: Neutral third-person observing the domestic ritual
refocalize.recommended_pov: Eldar's tightly controlled stillness (the novel explicitly follows his gaze being suppressed) — a brief insert shot of his clenched hands, then relief when the door closes. More cinematic, preserves the cultural note without stopping the scene.

refocalize.scene: Opening paragraph — narrator describes Hadji Murad's former glory as Shamil's naib vs. current fugitive state
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as Hadji Murad's own visceral present-tense experience: we see only what he sees — the faces he scans, the side street he chooses, the saklya he stops at. Former glory surfaces as a brief involuntary memory (visual flash) triggered by the old man's recognition, not narrator summary.

refocalize.scene: Old man waking, dressing, descending ladder — observed from outside
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's POV waiting impatiently below — tight on the old man's slow movements as seen through Hadji Murad's eyes, conveying urgency and concealment anxiety without cutting the warmth of the welcome.

refocalize.scene: Old man's cryptic response ('Habar yok' / 'He knows') and the eagle-hare fable
refocalize.current_pov: Neutral third-person narrator who glosses Hadji Murad's understanding ('Hadji Murad, understanding that the old man did not wish to say...')
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage through Hadji Murad's eyes: hold on his face as the old man speaks, let the camera read HM's quiet recognition. The narrator's explanatory gloss disappears — HM's slight nod carries the scene's meaning visually.

refocalize.scene: Eldar entering and mirroring Hadji Murad's weapon-hanging ritual
refocalize.current_pov: Neutral third-person, describes Eldar's actions sequentially after Hadji Murad's
refocalize.recommended_pov: Old man's POV watching both men: his slow eyes moving from Hadji Murad to Eldar establishes the hierarchy and the murid's loyalty in a single camera cut rather than prose description.

refocalize.scene: Russian ambush party night watch — Panov, Avdeev, Nikitin, Bondarenko smoke, gossip about cashbox, intercept Bata and Khan Mahoma
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as Bata's POV approaching Russian lines in darkness; the soldiers appear as silhouettes and threat, then as bumbling humans. Preserves Avdeev's humanity as surprise discovery rather than predicate. Alternatively cut the whole night-watch scene and show only the moment Vorontsov receives the envoy (reported in Chapter III).

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov drawing room card game and Poltoratsky's infatuation
refocalize.recommended_pov: If retained: restage as Hadji Murad later being told of the card-game world (e.g. Loris-Melikov describing Tiflis society) so the imperial frivolity is filtered through HM's alien perspective. Or cut entirely — HM's first meeting with Vorontsov at the Shalin glade carries the diplomatic weight without the drawing room prep.

refocalize.scene: Wood-felling officers' breakfast and Sleptsov heroism-myth discussion
refocalize.recommended_pov: Cut Sleptsov discussion. Retain Poltoratsky's elation-in-danger as a brief visual beat (him riding up cheerfully) before Avdeev is shot. The irony lands harder if it is action, not commentary.

refocalize.scene: Hadji Murad's forest camp prayer and dream of capturing Shamil
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep as HM POV — this is one of the rare direct-access moments into his hidden goal and ambition. Film can render via light/shadow on his face, the whistle signal, and a brief dream-image insert. Already protagonist POV — no refocalization needed.

refocalize.scene: Soldiers comment after the surrender procession rides past
refocalize.current_pov: Anonymous Russian soldiers on the road
refocalize.recommended_pov: Refocalize as Hadji Murad overhearing/sensing the soldiers' stares and muttering — his smile in return is already in the text. This keeps the chorus commentary while anchoring it in the protagonist's experience.

refocalize.scene: Poltoratsky's bedroom scene (post-card game, Tikhonov exchange, fantasy of Marya Vasilevna)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Cut. Zero information relevant to HM's trajectory. If the infatuation beat is needed, it is already implicit in the card-game scene.
